[Hampshire and Isle of Wight](/wiki/Hampshire_and_Isle_of_Wight_Fire_and_Rescue_Service "Hampshire and Isle of Wight Fire and Rescue Service")

([/ˈpɔːrtsməθ/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:En-uk-Portsmouth.ogg "File:En-uk-Portsmouth.ogg") [*PORTS-məth*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key")) is a [port](/wiki/Port "Port") [city](/wiki/City_status_in_the_United_Kingdom "City status in the United Kingdom") and [unitary authority](/wiki/Unitary_authority "Unitary authority") in [Hampshire](/wiki/Hampshire "Hampshire"), England. Most of Portsmouth is located on [Portsea Island](/wiki/Portsea_Island "Portsea Island"), off the south coast of England in the [Solent](/wiki/Solent "Solent"), making Portsmouth the only city in England not located primarily on the [mainland](/wiki/Great_Britain "Great Britain"). The city is located 22 miles (35 km) south-east of [Southampton](/wiki/Southampton "Southampton"), 50 miles (80 km) west of [Brighton and Hove](/wiki/Brighton_and_Hove "Brighton and Hove") and 74 miles (119 km) south-west of [London](/wiki/London "London"). With a population last recorded at 208,100, it is the most densely populated city in the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom").[[6]](#cite_note-auto-9) Portsmouth forms part of the [South Hampshire](/wiki/South_Hampshire "South Hampshire") urban area with [Gosport](/wiki/Gosport "Gosport"), [Fareham](/wiki/Borough_of_Fareham "Borough of Fareham"), [Havant](/wiki/Borough_of_Havant "Borough of Havant"), [Eastleigh](/wiki/Borough_of_Eastleigh "Borough of Eastleigh") and Southampton.

**Portsmouth** ([/ˈpɔːrtsməθ/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:En-uk-Portsmouth.ogg "File:En-uk-Portsmouth.ogg") [*PORTS-məth*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key")) is a [port](/wiki/Port "Port") [city](/wiki/City_status_in_the_United_Kingdom "City status in the United Kingdom") and [unitary authority](/wiki/Unitary_authority "Unitary authority") in [Hampshire](/wiki/Hampshire "Hampshire"), England. Most of Portsmouth is located on [Portsea Island](/wiki/Portsea_Island "Portsea Island"), off the south coast of England in the [Solent](/wiki/Solent "Solent"), making Portsmouth the only city in England not located primarily on the [mainland](/wiki/Great_Britain "Great Britain"). The city is located 22 miles (35 km) south-east of [Southampton](/wiki/Southampton "Southampton"), 50 miles (80 km) west of [Brighton and Hove](/wiki/Brighton_and_Hove "Brighton and Hove") and 74 miles (119 km) south-west of [London](/wiki/London "London"). With a population last recorded at 208,100, it is the most densely populated city in the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom").[[6]](#cite_note-auto-9) Portsmouth forms part of the [South Hampshire](/wiki/South_Hampshire "South Hampshire") urban area with [Gosport](/wiki/Gosport "Gosport"), [Fareham](/wiki/Borough_of_Fareham "Borough of Fareham"), [Havant](/wiki/Borough_of_Havant "Borough of Havant"), [Eastleigh](/wiki/Borough_of_Eastleigh "Borough of Eastleigh") and Southampton.

Portsmouth's history can be traced to [Roman times](/wiki/Roman_Britain "Roman Britain") and it has contained a significant Royal Navy dockyard and base for centuries. Portsmouth was founded c. 1180 by Anglo-Norman merchant [Jean de Gisors](/wiki/Jean_de_Gisors "Jean de Gisors") in the south-west area of Portsea Island, a location now known as [Old Portsmouth](/wiki/Old_Portsmouth "Old Portsmouth").[[7]](#cite_note-portsmouth-guide_co_uk-10) Around this time, de Gisors ordered the construction of a chapel dedicated to [St Thomas Becket](/wiki/Thomas_Becket "Thomas Becket").[[7]](#cite_note-portsmouth-guide_co_uk-10) This became a parish church by the 14th century. Portsmouth was established as a town with a royal charter on 2 May 1194.[[8]](#cite_note-history3-11)[[9]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEQuail199414–18-12) The city is home to the first drydock ever built. It was constructed by [Henry VII](/wiki/Henry_VII_of_England "Henry VII of England") in 1495.[[10]](#cite_note-13)

Portsmouth has the world's oldest [dry dock](/wiki/Dry_dock "Dry dock"), *"The Great Stone Dock"*; originally built in 1698, rebuilt in 1769 and presently known as "No.5 Dock".[[11]](#cite_note-14) The world's first mass [production line](/wiki/Production_line "Production line") was established at the naval base's [Block Mills](/wiki/Portsmouth_Block_Mills "Portsmouth Block Mills") which produced pulley blocks for the [Royal Navy](/wiki/Royal_Navy "Royal Navy") fleet. By the early-19th century, Portsmouth was the most heavily [fortified](/wiki/Fortification "Fortification") city in the world, and was considered "the world's greatest naval port" at the height of the [British Empire](/wiki/British_Empire "British Empire") throughout *[Pax Britannica](/wiki/Pax_Britannica "Pax Britannica")*. By 1859, a ring of defensive land and sea forts, known as the [Palmerston Forts](/wiki/Palmerston_Forts,_Portsmouth "Palmerston Forts, Portsmouth"), had been built around Portsmouth in anticipation of an invasion from continental Europe.

In the 20th century, Portsmouth achieved [city status](/wiki/City_status_in_the_United_Kingdom "City status in the United Kingdom") on 21 April 1926.[[12]](#cite_note-15) During the [Second World War](/wiki/Second_World_War "Second World War"), the city was bombed extensively in the [Portsmouth Blitz](/wiki/Portsmouth_Blitz "Portsmouth Blitz"), which resulted in the deaths of 930 people, and it was a pivotal embarkation point for the [D-Day landings](/wiki/D-Day_landings "D-Day landings"). In 1982, a large Royal Navy task force departed from Portsmouth for the [Falklands War](/wiki/Falklands_War "Falklands War"). [Her Majesty's Yacht *Britannia*](/wiki/HMY_Britannia "HMY Britannia") was formerly based in Portsmouth and oversaw the [transfer of Hong Kong](/wiki/Transfer_of_sovereignty_over_Hong_Kong "Transfer of sovereignty over Hong Kong") in 1997, after which *Britannia* was retired from royal service, decommissioned and relocated to [Leith](/wiki/Leith "Leith") as a museum ship.

[HMNB Portsmouth](/wiki/HMNB_Portsmouth "HMNB Portsmouth") is an operational Royal Navy base and is home to two-thirds of the UK's surface fleet. The base has long been nicknamed "Pompey", a nickname it shares with the wider city of Portsmouth and [Portsmouth Football Club](/wiki/Portsmouth_Football_Club "Portsmouth Football Club"). The naval base also contains the [National Museum of the Royal Navy](/wiki/National_Museum_of_the_Royal_Navy "National Museum of the Royal Navy") and [Portsmouth Historic Dockyard](/wiki/Portsmouth_Historic_Dockyard "Portsmouth Historic Dockyard"); which has a collection of historic warships, including the *Mary Rose*, [Lord Nelson](/wiki/Horatio_Nelson,_1st_Viscount_Nelson "Horatio Nelson, 1st Viscount Nelson")'s flagship, [HMS *Victory*](/wiki/HMS_Victory "HMS Victory") (the world's oldest naval ship still in commission), and [HMS *Warrior*](/wiki/HMS_Warrior_(1860) "HMS Warrior (1860)"), the Royal Navy's first [ironclad warship](/wiki/Ironclad_warship "Ironclad warship").

The former [HMS *Vernon*](/wiki/HMS_Vernon_(shore_establishment) "HMS Vernon (shore establishment)") shore establishment has been redeveloped into a large retail [outlet](/wiki/Outlet_store "Outlet store") destination known as [Gunwharf Quays](/wiki/Gunwharf_Quays "Gunwharf Quays") which opened in 2001.[[13]](#cite_note-16) Portsmouth is among the few British cities with two cathedrals: the [Anglican Cathedral of St Thomas](/wiki/Portsmouth_Cathedral "Portsmouth Cathedral") and the [Roman Catholic](/wiki/Roman_Catholic "Roman Catholic") [Cathedral of St John the Evangelist](/wiki/Cathedral_of_St_John_the_Evangelist,_Portsmouth "Cathedral of St John the Evangelist, Portsmouth"). The waterfront and [Portsmouth Harbour](/wiki/Portsmouth_Harbour "Portsmouth Harbour") are dominated by the [Spinnaker Tower](/wiki/Spinnaker_Tower "Spinnaker Tower"), one of the [United Kingdom's tallest structures](/wiki/List_of_tallest_structures_in_the_United_Kingdom "List of tallest structures in the United Kingdom") at 560 feet (170 m).

[Southsea](/wiki/Southsea "Southsea") is Portsmouth's [seaside resort](/wiki/British_coastal_resort "British coastal resort"), which was named after Southsea Castle. Southsea has two piers; [Clarence Pier](/wiki/Clarence_Pier "Clarence Pier") amusement park and [South Parade Pier](/wiki/South_Parade_Pier "South Parade Pier"). The world's only regular hovercraft service operates from [Southsea Hoverport](/wiki/Southsea_Hoverport "Southsea Hoverport") to [Ryde](/wiki/Ryde "Ryde") on the [Isle of Wight](/wiki/Isle_of_Wight "Isle of Wight"). [Southsea Common](/wiki/Southsea_Common "Southsea Common") is a large open-air public recreation space which serves as a venue for a wide variety of annual events.

The city has several mainline railway stations that connect to [London Victoria](/wiki/London_Victoria_railway_station "London Victoria railway station") and [London Waterloo](/wiki/London_Waterloo_railway_station "London Waterloo railway station") amongst other lines in southern England. [Portsmouth International Port](/wiki/Portsmouth_International_Port "Portsmouth International Port") is a commercial cruise ship and ferry port for international destinations. The port is the second busiest in the United Kingdom after [Dover](/wiki/Port_of_Dover "Port of Dover"), handling around three million passengers a year. The city formerly had its own airport, [Portsmouth Airport](/wiki/Portsmouth_Airport_(Hampshire) "Portsmouth Airport (Hampshire)"), until its closure in 1973. The [University of Portsmouth](/wiki/University_of_Portsmouth "University of Portsmouth") enrolled 23,000 students.

Portsmouth is the birthplace of notable people such as author [Charles Dickens](/wiki/Charles_Dickens "Charles Dickens"), engineer [Isambard Kingdom Brunel](/wiki/Isambard_Kingdom_Brunel "Isambard Kingdom Brunel"), former Prime Minister [James Callaghan](/wiki/James_Callaghan "James Callaghan"), actor [Peter Sellers](/wiki/Peter_Sellers "Peter Sellers") and author-journalist [Christopher Hitchens](/wiki/Christopher_Hitchens "Christopher Hitchens").

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Portsmouth&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

Main article: [History of Portsmouth](/wiki/History_of_Portsmouth "History of Portsmouth")

### Early history

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Portsmouth&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Early history")]

The [Romans](/wiki/Roman_Britain "Roman Britain") built [Portus Adurni](/wiki/Portus_Adurni "Portus Adurni") (now called [Portchester Castle](/wiki/Portchester_Castle "Portchester Castle")), a [fort](/wiki/Castra "Castra"), at nearby [Portchester](/wiki/Portchester "Portchester") in the late 3rd century.[[14]](#cite_note-17) The city's [Old English](/wiki/Old_English "Old English") (Anglo-Saxon) name, "Portesmuða", is derived from *port* (a haven) and *muða* (the mouth of a large river or estuary).[[15]](#cite_note-18) In the *[Anglo-Saxon Chronicle](/wiki/Anglo-Saxon_Chronicle "Anglo-Saxon Chronicle")*, a warrior named Port and his two sons killed a noble Briton in Portsmouth in 501.[[16]](#cite_note-19) [Winston Churchill](/wiki/Winston_Churchill "Winston Churchill"), in *[A History of the English-Speaking Peoples](/wiki/A_History_of_the_English-Speaking_Peoples "A History of the English-Speaking Peoples")*, wrote that Port was a pirate who founded Portsmouth in 501.[[17]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChurchill201541-20)[[18]](#cite_note-21)

England's southern coast was vulnerable to [Danish Viking](/wiki/Danes_(Germanic_tribe) "Danes (Germanic tribe)") invasions during the eighth and ninth centuries, and was conquered by Danish pirates in 787.[[19]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAllen201526-22) In 838, during the reign of [Æthelwulf, King of Wessex](/wiki/%C3%86thelwulf,_King_of_Wessex "Æthelwulf, King of Wessex"), a Danish fleet landed between Portsmouth and Southampton and plundered the region.[[20]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAllen201527-23) Æthelwulf sent Wulfherd and the governor of [Dorsetshire](/wiki/Dorset "Dorset") to confront the Danes at Portsmouth, where most of their ships were docked. Although the Danes were driven off, Wulfherd was killed.[[20]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAllen201527-23) The Danes returned in 1001 and pillaged Portsmouth and the surrounding area, threatening the English with extinction.[[21]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAllen201529-24)[[22]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAllen201530-25) They were massacred by the English survivors the following year; rebuilding began, although the town experienced further attacks until [1066](/wiki/Battle_of_Stamford_Bridge "Battle of Stamford Bridge").[[23]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAllen201531-26)

### Norman to Tudor

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Portsmouth&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Norman to Tudor")]

[![A front facing view of Portsmouth's Round Tower, which once guarded the entrance to Portsmouth Harbour. The Round Tower itself is made of stone and has a large circular base.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/92/Round_Tower_%28Portsmouth%292009.jpg/250px-Round_Tower_%28Portsmouth%292009.jpg)](/wiki/File:Round_Tower_(Portsmouth)2009.jpg)

The [Round Tower](/wiki/Round_Tower_(Portsmouth) "Round Tower (Portsmouth)") was built in 1418 to defend the entrance to [Portsmouth Harbour](/wiki/Portsmouth_Harbour "Portsmouth Harbour").

Although Portsmouth was not mentioned in the 1086 [Domesday Book](/wiki/Domesday_Book "Domesday Book"), *Bocheland* ([Buckland](/wiki/Buckland,_Portsmouth "Buckland, Portsmouth")), *Copenore* ([Copnor](/wiki/Copnor "Copnor")), and *Frodentone* ([Fratton](/wiki/Fratton "Fratton")) were.[[8]](#cite_note-history3-11) According to some sources, it was founded in 1180 by the Anglo-Norman merchant [Jean de Gisors](/wiki/Jean_de_Gisors "Jean de Gisors").[[24]](#cite_note-history2-27)

King [Henry II](/wiki/Henry_II_of_England "Henry II of England") died in 1189; his son, [Richard I](/wiki/Richard_I_of_England "Richard I of England") (who had spent most of his life in France), arrived in Portsmouth en route to his coronation in London.[[25]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAllen201532-28) When Richard returned from captivity in [Austria](/wiki/Duchy_of_Austria "Duchy of Austria") in May 1194, he summoned an army and a fleet of 100 ships to the port.[[26]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAllen201533-29) Richard gave Portsmouth market-town status with a royal charter on 2 May, authorising an annual fifteen-day free-market fair, weekly markets and a local court to deal with minor matters, and exempted its inhabitants from an £18 annual tax.[[8]](#cite_note-history3-11)[[9]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEQuail199414–18-12) The 1194 royal charter's 800th anniversary was celebrated in 1994 with ceremonies at the city museum.[[27]](#cite_note-30)

[King John](/wiki/John,_King_of_England "John, King of England") reaffirmed Richard I's rights and privileges, and established a permanent naval base. The first docks were begun by [William of Wrotham](/wiki/William_of_Wrotham "William of Wrotham") in 1212,[[8]](#cite_note-history3-11)[[26]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAllen201533-29) and John summoned his earls, barons, and military advisers to plan an invasion of [Normandy](/wiki/Duchy_of_Normandy "Duchy of Normandy").[[28]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAllen201534-31) In 1229, declaring war against France, [Henry III](/wiki/Henry_III_of_England "Henry III of England") assembled a force described by historian Lake Allen as "one of the finest armies that had ever been raised in England".[[29]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAllen201536-32) The invasion stalled, and returned from France in October 1231.[[30]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAllen201537-33) Henry III summoned troops to invade [Guienne](/wiki/Guienne "Guienne") in 1242, and [Edward I](/wiki/Edward_I_of_England "Edward I of England") sent supplies for his army in France in 1295.[[31]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAllen201537,_39-34) Commercial interests had grown by the following century, and its exports included wool, corn, grain, and livestock.[[32]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAllen201543-35)

[Edward II](/wiki/Edward_II "Edward II") ordered all ports on the south coast to assemble their largest vessels at Portsmouth to carry soldiers and horses to the [Duchy of Aquitaine](/wiki/Duchy_of_Aquitaine "Duchy of Aquitaine") in 1324 to strengthen defences.[[33]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAllen201544-36) A French fleet commanded by [David II of Scotland](/wiki/David_II_of_Scotland "David II of Scotland") attacked in the [English Channel](/wiki/English_Channel "English Channel"), ransacked the [Isle of Wight](/wiki/Isle_of_Wight "Isle of Wight") and threatened the town. [Edward III](/wiki/Edward_III_of_England "Edward III of England") instructed all maritime towns to build vessels and raise troops to rendezvous at Portsmouth.[[33]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAllen201544-36) Two years later, a French fleet led by [Nicholas Béhuchet](/wiki/Nicholas_B%C3%A9huchet "Nicholas Béhuchet") [raided Portsmouth](/wiki/Raid_on_Portsmouth_(1338) "Raid on Portsmouth (1338)") and destroyed most of the town; only the stone-built church and hospital survived.[[34]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESumption1990395,_396-37)[[35]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESeward1988-38)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*] After the raid, Edward III exempted the town from national taxes to aid its reconstruction.[[36]](#cite_note-porthis-39) In 1377, shortly after Edward died, the French landed in Portsmouth. Although the town was plundered and burnt, its inhabitants drove the French off to raid towns in the [West Country](/wiki/West_Country "West Country").[[37]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAllen201548-40)

[![A black and white map of Portsmouth dated around 1540](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c1/Map_of_portsmouth_a_bit_before_1540.PNG/250px-Map_of_portsmouth_a_bit_before_1540.PNG)](/wiki/File:Map_of_portsmouth_a_bit_before_1540.PNG)

Portsmouth c. 1540

[Henry V](/wiki/Henry_V_of_England "Henry V of England") gathered his forces in Portsmouth for an invasion of France in 1415, it was while staying at Portchester Castle that the Southampton plot was uncovered. This campaign would culminate with victory at the battle of Agincourt.[[38]](#cite_note-41) He also built Portsmouth's first permanent [fortifications](/wiki/Fortifications_of_Portsmouth "Fortifications of Portsmouth"). In 1416, a number of French ships blockaded the town (which housed ships which were set to invade Normandy); Henry gathered a fleet at Southampton, and invaded the Norman coast in August that year.[[39]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAllen201549-42) Recognising the town's growing importance, he ordered a wooden [Round Tower](/wiki/Round_Tower_(Portsmouth) "Round Tower (Portsmouth)") to be built at the mouth of the harbour; it was completed in 1426.[[40]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHewitt201327-43) [Henry VII](/wiki/Henry_VII_of_England "Henry VII of England") rebuilt the fortifications with stone, assisted Robert Brygandine and Sir [Reginald Bray](/wiki/Reginald_Bray "Reginald Bray") in the construction of the world's first [dry dock](/wiki/Dry_dock "Dry dock"),[[41]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHewitt201333-44) and raised the [Square Tower](/wiki/Square_Tower "Square Tower") in 1494.[[40]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHewitt201327-43) He made Portsmouth a Royal Dockyard, England's only dockyard considered "national".[[42]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAllen201553-45) Although [King Alfred](/wiki/Alfred_the_Great "Alfred the Great") may have used Portsmouth to build ships as early as the ninth century, the first warship recorded as constructed in the town was the [*Sweepstake*](/w/index.php?title=Sweepstake_(warship)&action=edit&redlink=1 "Sweepstake (warship) (page does not exist)") (built in 1497).[[43]](#cite_note-46)

[Henry VIII](/wiki/Henry_VIII_of_England "Henry VIII of England") built [Southsea Castle](/wiki/Southsea_Castle "Southsea Castle"), financed by the [Dissolution of the Monasteries](/wiki/Dissolution_of_the_Monasteries "Dissolution of the Monasteries"), in 1539 in anticipation of a French invasion.[[44]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAllen2015143-47)[[45]](#cite_note-48) He also invested heavily in the town's dockyard, expanding it to 8 acres (3.2 ha).[[46]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHewitt201323-49) Around this time, a Tudor [defensive boom](/wiki/Boom_(navigational_barrier) "Boom (navigational barrier)") stretched from the Round Tower to Fort Blockhouse in Gosport to protect Portsmouth Harbour.[[47]](#cite_note-boom-50)

From Southsea Castle, Henry witnessed his flagship *Mary Rose* sink in action against the French fleet in the 1545 [Battle of the Solent](/wiki/Battle_of_the_Solent "Battle of the Solent") with the loss of about 500 lives.[[48]](#cite_note-51) Some historians believe that the *Mary Rose* turned too quickly and submerged her open gun ports; according to others, it sank due to poor design.[[49]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHewitt201337-52) Portsmouth's fortifications were improved by successive monarchs. The town experienced an outbreak of [plague](/wiki/Plague_(disease) "Plague (disease)") in 1563, which killed about 300 of its 2,000 inhabitants.[[24]](#cite_note-history2-27)

### Stuart to Georgian

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Portsmouth&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Stuart to Georgian")]

[![A view of Old Portsmouth taken from the viewing deck of the Spinnaker Tower. Old buildings, cobbled streets and a small island can be seen in the frame.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8a/Old_Portsmouth.jpg/250px-Old_Portsmouth.jpg)](/wiki/File:Old_Portsmouth.jpg)

View of [Old Portsmouth](/wiki/Old_Portsmouth "Old Portsmouth") from the [Spinnaker Tower](/wiki/Spinnaker_Tower "Spinnaker Tower")

In 1623, [Charles I](/wiki/Charles_I_of_England "Charles I of England") (then Prince of Wales) returned to Portsmouth from France and Spain.[[50]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAllen201554-53) His unpopular military adviser, [George Villiers, 1st Duke of Buckingham](/wiki/George_Villiers,_1st_Duke_of_Buckingham "George Villiers, 1st Duke of Buckingham"), was stabbed to death in an [Old Portsmouth](/wiki/Old_Portsmouth "Old Portsmouth") pub by war veteran [John Felton](/wiki/John_Felton_(assassin) "John Felton (assassin)") five years later.[[8]](#cite_note-history3-11)[[51]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAllen201554,_55-54) Felton never attempted to escape, and was caught walking the streets when soldiers confronted him; he said, "I know that he is dead, for I had the force of forty men when I struck the blow".[[52]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAllen201556-55) Felton was hanged, and his body chained to a [gibbet](/wiki/Gibbet "Gibbet") on Southsea Common as a warning to others.[[24]](#cite_note-history2-27)[[52]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAllen201556-55) The murder took place in the Greyhound public house on High Street, which is now Buckingham House and has a commemorative plaque.[[53]](#cite_note-56)

Most residents (including the mayor) supported the [parliamentarians](/wiki/Roundhead "Roundhead") during the [English Civil War](/wiki/English_Civil_War "English Civil War"), although military governor [Colonel Goring](/wiki/George_Goring,_Lord_Goring "George Goring, Lord Goring") supported the [royalists](/wiki/Cavalier "Cavalier").[[24]](#cite_note-history2-27) The town, a base of the parliamentarian navy, was blockaded from the sea. Parliamentarian troops were sent to [besiege it](/wiki/Siege_of_Portsmouth "Siege of Portsmouth"), and the guns of Southsea Castle were fired at the town's royalist garrison. Parliamentarians in [Gosport](/wiki/Gosport "Gosport") joined the assault, damaging [St Thomas's Church](/wiki/Portsmouth_Cathedral "Portsmouth Cathedral").[[24]](#cite_note-history2-27)[[54]](#cite_note-siege-57) On 5 September 1642, the remaining royalists in the garrison at the Square Tower were forced to surrender after Goring threatened to blow it up; he and his garrison were allowed safe passage out of the city.[[54]](#cite_note-siege-57)[[55]](#cite_note-siege1-58)

After the end of the [Civil War](/wiki/English_Civil_War "English Civil War"), Portsmouth was among the first towns to declare [Charles II](/wiki/Charles_II_of_England "Charles II of England") king and began to prosper.[[56]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAllen201557-59) The first ship built in over 100 years, [*Portsmouth*](/wiki/English_ship_Portsmouth_(1650) "English ship Portsmouth (1650)"), was launched in 1650; twelve ships were built between 1650 and 1660. Under the [Commonwealth of England](/wiki/Commonwealth_of_England "Commonwealth of England"), [Robert Blake](/wiki/Robert_Blake_(admiral) "Robert Blake (admiral)") used the harbour as his base during the [First Anglo-Dutch War](/wiki/First_Anglo-Dutch_War "First Anglo-Dutch War") in 1652 and the [Anglo-Spanish War](/wiki/Anglo-Spanish_War_(1654%E2%80%931660) "Anglo-Spanish War (1654–1660)"). He died within sight of the town, returning from [Cádiz](/wiki/C%C3%A1diz "Cádiz") in 1657.[[55]](#cite_note-siege1-58) After [the Restoration](/wiki/Stuart_Restoration "Stuart Restoration"), Charles II married [Catherine of Braganza](/wiki/Catherine_of_Braganza "Catherine of Braganza") at the [Royal Garrison Church](/wiki/Domus_Dei "Domus Dei") on 14 May 1662.[[57]](#cite_note-garr-60)[[58]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHewitt201357-61) Catherine was reputed to have introduced the cultural practice of [tea drinking](/wiki/Tea_culture#United_Kingdom "Tea culture") to England at this event.[[59]](#cite_note-62)

During the late 17th century, Portsmouth continued to grow; a new wharf was constructed in 1663 for military use, and a [mast](/wiki/Mast_(sailing) "Mast (sailing)") pond was dug in 1665. In 1684, a list of ships docked in Portsmouth was evidence of its increasing national importance.[[60]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAllen201558-63) Between 1667 and 1685, the town's fortifications were rebuilt; new walls were constructed with [bastions](/wiki/Bastion "Bastion") and two moats were dug, making Portsmouth one of the world's most heavily fortified places.[[24]](#cite_note-history2-27)

In 1759, General [James Wolfe](/wiki/James_Wolfe "James Wolfe") sailed to capture [Quebec](/wiki/Quebec "Quebec"); the expedition, although successful, cost him his life. His body was brought back to Portsmouth in November, and received high naval and military honours.[[61]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAllen201565-64) Two years later, on 30 May 1775, Captain [James Cook](/wiki/James_Cook "James Cook") arrived on [HMS *Endeavour*](/wiki/HMS_Endeavour "HMS Endeavour") after circumnavigating the globe.[[8]](#cite_note-history3-11)[[62]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECollingridge2003311-65) The 11-ship [First Fleet](/wiki/First_Fleet "First Fleet") left on 13 May 1787 to establish the first European colony in [Australia](/wiki/Australia "Australia"), the beginning of prisoner transportation;[[63]](#cite_note-Gutenberg_FF-66)[[64]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFrost2012165-67) Captain [William Bligh](/wiki/William_Bligh "William Bligh") of [HMS *Bounty*](/wiki/HMS_Bounty "HMS Bounty") also sailed from the harbour that year.[[8]](#cite_note-history3-11)[[65]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHewitt2013223-68) After the 28 April 1789 [mutiny on the *Bounty*](/wiki/Mutiny_on_the_Bounty "Mutiny on the Bounty"), [HMS *Pandora*](/wiki/HMS_Pandora_(1779) "HMS Pandora (1779)") was dispatched from Portsmouth to bring the mutineers back for trial. The [court-martial](/wiki/Court-martial "Court-martial") opened on 12 September 1792 aboard [HMS *Duke*](/wiki/HMS_Duke_(1777) "HMS Duke (1777)") in Portsmouth Harbour; of the ten remaining men, three were sentenced to death.[[66]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHewitt2013223,_224-69)[[67]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHough1972276-70) In 1789, a chapel was erected in Prince George's Street and was dedicated to [St John](/wiki/John_the_Apostle "John the Apostle") by the Bishop of Winchester. Around this time, a [bill](/wiki/Bill_(law) "Bill (law)") was passed in the House of Commons on the creation of a canal to link Portsmouth to Chichester; however, the project was abandoned.[[68]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAllen2015130-71)

The city's nickname, Pompey, is thought to have derived from the log entry of [Portsmouth Point](/wiki/Portsmouth_Point "Portsmouth Point") (contracted "Po'm.P." – *Po'*rts*m*outh *P.*oint) as ships entered the harbour; navigational charts use the contraction.[[69]](#cite_note-72) According to one historian, the name may have been brought back from a group of Portsmouth-based sailors who visited [Pompey's Pillar](/wiki/Pompey%27s_Pillar_(column) "Pompey's Pillar (column)") in [Alexandria](/wiki/Alexandria "Alexandria"), Egypt, around 1781.[[70]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHewitt201398-73) Another theory is that it is named after the harbour's guardship, [*Pompee*](/wiki/HMS_Pompee "HMS Pompee"), a 74-gun French [ship of the line](/wiki/Ship_of_the_line "Ship of the line") captured in 1793.[[71]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBreverton2010282-74)

Portsmouth's coat of arms is attested in the early 19th century as "azure a crescent or, surmounted by an estoile of eight points of the last."[[72]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBerryGlover1828-75)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*] Its design is apparently based on 18th-century mayoral seals.[[73]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEast1891656-76) A connection of the coat of arms with the Great Seal of Richard I (which had a separate star and crescent) dates to the 20th century.[[74]](#cite_note-77)

### Industrial Revolution to Edwardian

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Portsmouth&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Industrial Revolution to Edwardian")]

[![A picture of the iron-clad HMS Warrior docked in Portsmouth's historic harbour. The ship has since been restored to its original Victorian condition.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/aa/HMS_Warrior_at_Midday._-_panoramio.jpg/250px-HMS_Warrior_at_Midday._-_panoramio.jpg)](/wiki/File:HMS_Warrior_at_Midday._-_panoramio.jpg)

[HMS *Warrior*](/wiki/HMS_Warrior_(1860) "HMS Warrior (1860)") (launched in 1860) has been restored to its original Victorian condition.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4c/P277_View_of_Portsmouth_Harbour%2C_as_it_appears_at_the_present_time.jpg/250px-P277_View_of_Portsmouth_Harbour%2C_as_it_appears_at_the_present_time.jpg)](/wiki/File:P277_View_of_Portsmouth_Harbour,_as_it_appears_at_the_present_time.jpg)

View of Portsmouth harbour about 1860

[Marc Isambard Brunel](/wiki/Marc_Isambard_Brunel "Marc Isambard Brunel") established the world's first mass-production line at [Portsmouth Block Mills](/wiki/Portsmouth_Block_Mills "Portsmouth Block Mills"), making [pulley](/wiki/Pulley "Pulley") [blocks](/wiki/Block_(sailing) "Block (sailing)") for [rigging](/wiki/Rigging "Rigging") on the navy's ships.[[75]](#cite_note-royal-78) The first machines were installed in January 1803, and the final set (for large blocks) in March 1805. In 1808, the mills produced 130,000 blocks.[[76]](#cite_note-block-79) By the turn of the 19th century, Portsmouth was the largest industrial site in the world; it had a workforce of 8,000, and an annual budget of £570,000.[[77]](#cite_note-80)

In 1805, [Admiral Nelson](/wiki/Horatio_Nelson "Horatio Nelson") left Portsmouth to command the fleet which defeated France and Spain at the [Battle of Trafalgar](/wiki/Battle_of_Trafalgar "Battle of Trafalgar").[[8]](#cite_note-history3-11) The Royal Navy's reliance on Portsmouth led to its becoming the most fortified city in the world.[[78]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPevsner1967422-81) The Royal Navy's [West Africa Squadron](/wiki/West_Africa_Squadron "West Africa Squadron"), tasked with halting the slave trade, began operating out of Portsmouth in 1808.[[79]](#cite_note-BBC_slave_trade-82) A network of forts, known as the [Palmerston Forts](/wiki/Palmerston_Forts,_Portsmouth "Palmerston Forts, Portsmouth"), was built around the town as part of a programme led by Prime Minister [Lord Palmerston](/wiki/Lord_Palmerston "Lord Palmerston") to defend British military bases from an inland attack following an Anglo-French war scare in 1859. The forts were nicknamed "Palmerston's Follies" because their armaments were pointed inland and not out to sea.[[80]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHewitt201379-83)

In April 1811, the Portsea Island Company constructed the first piped-water supply[[81]](#cite_note-water-84) to upper- and middle-class houses.[[24]](#cite_note-history2-27) It supplied water to about 4,500 of Portsmouth's 14,000 houses, generating an income of £5,000 a year.[[81]](#cite_note-water-84) HMS *Victory*'s active career ended in 1812, when she was moored in Portsmouth Harbour and used as a [depot ship](/wiki/Depot_ship "Depot ship"). The town of Gosport contributed £75 a year to the ship's maintenance.[[82]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHewitt201339-85) In 1818, [John Pounds](/wiki/John_Pounds "John Pounds") began teaching working-class children in the country's first [ragged school](/wiki/Ragged_school "Ragged school").[[83]](#cite_note-86)[[84]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHewitt201366,_67-87) The Portsea Improvement Commissioners installed gas street lighting throughout Portsmouth in 1820,[[8]](#cite_note-history3-11) followed by Old Portsmouth three years later.[[24]](#cite_note-history2-27)

During the 19th century, Portsmouth expanded across Portsea Island. [Buckland](/wiki/Buckland,_Portsmouth "Buckland, Portsmouth") was merged into the town by the 1860s, and Fratton and [Stamshaw](/wiki/Stamshaw "Stamshaw") were incorporated by the next decade. Between 1865 and 1870, the council built sewers after more than 800 people died in a [cholera](/wiki/Cholera "Cholera") epidemic; according to a [by-law](/wiki/By-law "By-law"), any house within 100 feet (30 m) of a sewer had to be connected to it.[[8]](#cite_note-history3-11) By 1871 the population had risen to 100,000,[[24]](#cite_note-history2-27) and the national census listed Portsmouth's population as 113,569.[[8]](#cite_note-history3-11) A working-class suburb was constructed in the 1870s, when about 1,820 houses were built, and it became [Somerstown](/wiki/Somerstown,_Hampshire "Somerstown, Hampshire").[[8]](#cite_note-history3-11) Despite public-health improvements, 514 people died in an 1872 [smallpox](/wiki/Smallpox "Smallpox") epidemic.[[8]](#cite_note-history3-11) On 21 December of that year, the [*Challenger* expedition](/wiki/Challenger_expedition "Challenger expedition") embarked on a 68,890-nautical-mile (127,580 km) circumnavigation of the globe for scientific research.[[85]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERice199927–48-88)[[86]](#cite_note-89)

When the [British Empire](/wiki/British_Empire "British Empire") was at its height of power, covering a quarter of Earth's total land area and 458 million people at the turn of the 20th century, Portsmouth was considered "the world's greatest naval port".[[87]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHewitt201324-90) In 1900, Portsmouth Dockyard employed 8,000 people – a figure which increased to 23,000 during the [First World War](/wiki/First_World_War "First World War").[[24]](#cite_note-history2-27)[[88]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHewitt201391-91) The whole of Portsea Island came united under the control of Portsmouth borough council in 1904.[[89]](#cite_note-portsenc-92)

In 1906, [HMS *Dreadnought*](/wiki/HMS_Dreadnought_(1906) "HMS Dreadnought (1906)") was launched from Portsmouth Dockyard.[[90]](#cite_note-93) The ship revolutionised naval warfare and began an arms race with Germany. The ship's entry into service in 1906 represented such an advance in naval technology that her name came to be associated with an entire generation of battleships.

### 1913 terrorist attack

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Portsmouth&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: 1913 terrorist attack")]

See also: [Suffragette bombing and arson campaign](/wiki/Suffragette_bombing_and_arson_campaign "Suffragette bombing and arson campaign")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b3/Portsmouth_Semaphore_Tower.jpg/250px-Portsmouth_Semaphore_Tower.jpg)](/wiki/File:Portsmouth_Semaphore_Tower.jpg)

A fire started by [suffragettes](/wiki/Suffragettes "Suffragettes") at the [semaphore tower](/wiki/Semaphore_tower "Semaphore tower"), [Portsmouth dockyard](/wiki/Portsmouth_dockyard "Portsmouth dockyard"), in December 1913, killed two men.

A major [terrorist](/wiki/Terrorist "Terrorist") incident occurred in the city in 1913, which led to the deaths of two men. During the [suffragette bombing and arson campaign](/wiki/Suffragette_bombing_and_arson_campaign "Suffragette bombing and arson campaign") of 1912–1914, [militant](/wiki/Militant "Militant") [suffragettes](/wiki/Suffragettes "Suffragettes") of the [Women's Social and Political Union](/wiki/Women%27s_Social_and_Political_Union "Women's Social and Political Union") carried out a series of politically motivated bombing and arson attacks nationwide as part of their campaign for [women's suffrage](/wiki/Women%27s_suffrage "Women's suffrage").[[91]](#cite_note-94) In one of the more serious suffragette attacks, a fire was purposely started at [Portsmouth dockyard](/wiki/Portsmouth_dockyard "Portsmouth dockyard") on 20 December 1913, in which two sailors were killed after it spread through the industrial area.[[92]](#cite_note-Portsmouth-95)[[93]](#cite_note-Webb-96)[[94]](#cite_note-Bearman-97) The fire spread rapidly as there were many old wooden buildings in the area, including the historic semaphore tower which dated back to the eighteenth century, which was completely destroyed.[[93]](#cite_note-Webb-96) The damage to the dockyard area cost the city £200,000 in damages, equivalent to £23,600,000 today.[[93]](#cite_note-Webb-96) In the midst of the firestorm, a battleship, [HMS *Queen Mary*](/wiki/HMS_Queen_Mary "HMS Queen Mary"), had to be towed to safety to avoid the flames.[[93]](#cite_note-Webb-96) The two victims were a pensioner and a signalman.[[93]](#cite_note-Webb-96)

The attack was notable enough to be reported on in the press in the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States"), with the *[New York Times](/wiki/New_York_Times "New York Times")* reporting on the disaster two days after with the headline "Big Portsmouth Fire Loss".[[92]](#cite_note-Portsmouth-95) The report also disclosed that at a previous police raid on a suffragette headquarters, "papers were discovered disclosing a plan to fire the yard".[[92]](#cite_note-Portsmouth-95)

### First and Second World Wars

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Portsmouth&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: First and Second World Wars")]

[![In this photograph, King George VI is inspecting the crew of the Norwegian ship HNoMS Draug, which was docked in Portsmouth sometime during the war.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/33/George_VI_inspecting_the_crew_of_HNoMS_Draug.jpg/250px-George_VI_inspecting_the_crew_of_HNoMS_Draug.jpg)](/wiki/File:George_VI_inspecting_the_crew_of_HNoMS_Draug.jpg)

[George VI](/wiki/George_VI "George VI") inspecting the crew of the [HNoMS *Draug*](/wiki/HNoMS_Draug_(1908) "HNoMS Draug (1908)") in Portsmouth during the Second World War

On 1 October 1916, Portsmouth was bombed by a [Zeppelin](/wiki/Zeppelin "Zeppelin") airship.[[95]](#cite_note-nazi1-98) Although the [Oberste Heeresleitung](/wiki/Oberste_Heeresleitung "Oberste Heeresleitung") (German Supreme Army Command) said that the town was "lavishly bombarded with good results", there were no reports of bombs dropped in the area.[[96]](#cite_note-99) According to another source, the bombs were mistakenly dropped into the harbour rather than the dockyard.[[95]](#cite_note-nazi1-98) About 1,200 ships were refitted in the dockyard during the war, making it one of the empire's most strategic ports at the time.[[88]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHewitt201391-91)

Portsmouth's boundaries were extended onto the mainland of [Great Britain](/wiki/Great_Britain "Great Britain") between 1920 and 1932 by incorporating [Paulsgrove](/wiki/Paulsgrove "Paulsgrove"), [Wymering](/wiki/Wymering "Wymering"), [Cosham](/wiki/Cosham "Cosham"), [Drayton](/wiki/Drayton,_Hampshire "Drayton, Hampshire") and [Farlington](/wiki/Farlington,_Hampshire "Farlington, Hampshire") into Portsmouth.[[89]](#cite_note-portsenc-92) Portsmouth was granted [city status](/wiki/City_status_in_the_United_Kingdom "City status in the United Kingdom") in 1926 after a long campaign by the borough council.[[89]](#cite_note-portsenc-92) The application was made on the grounds that it was the "first naval port of the kingdom".[[97]](#cite_note-100) In 1929, the city council added the [motto](/wiki/Motto "Motto") "Heaven's Light Our Guide" to the medieval coat of arms. Except for the celestial objects in the arms, the motto was that of the [Star of India](/wiki/Order_of_the_Star_of_India "Order of the Star of India") and referred to the troopships bound for [British India](/wiki/British_India "British India") which left from the port.[[98]](#cite_note-blu-101) The crest and [supporters](/wiki/Supporter "Supporter") are based on those of the [royal arms](/wiki/Royal_arms_of_the_United_Kingdom "Royal arms of the United Kingdom"), but altered to show the city's maritime connections: the lions and unicorn have fish tails, and a [naval crown](/wiki/Naval_crown "Naval crown") and a representation of the Tudor defensive boom which stretched across Portsmouth Harbour are around the unicorn's neck.[[47]](#cite_note-boom-50)[[98]](#cite_note-blu-101)

During the [Second World War](/wiki/Second_World_War "Second World War"), the city (particularly the port) was bombed extensively by the [Luftwaffe](/wiki/Luftwaffe "Luftwaffe") in the Portsmouth Blitz.[[8]](#cite_note-history3-11) Portsmouth experienced 67 air raids between July 1940 and May 1944, which destroyed 6,625 houses and severely damaged 6,549.[[24]](#cite_note-history2-27) The air raids caused 930 deaths and wounded almost 3,000 people,[[99]](#cite_note-guild-102)[[100]](#cite_note-103) many in the dockyard and military establishments.[[101]](#cite_note-104) On the night of the city's heaviest raid (10 January 1941), the Luftwaffe dropped 140 tonnes of high-explosive bombs which killed 171 people and left 3,000 homeless.[[102]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHewitt2013151-105) Many of the city's houses were damaged, and areas of [Landport](/wiki/Landport "Landport") and Old Portsmouth destroyed; the future site of [Gunwharf Quays](/wiki/Gunwharf_Quays "Gunwharf Quays") was razed to the ground.[[103]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHewitt2013186-106) The [Guildhall](/wiki/Portsmouth_Guildhall "Portsmouth Guildhall") was hit by an incendiary bomb which burnt out the interior and destroyed its inner walls,[[104]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHewitt2013147-107) although the civic plate was retrieved unharmed from the vault under the front steps.[[99]](#cite_note-guild-102) After the raid, Portsmouth mayor Denis Daley wrote for the *Evening News*:

> We are bruised but we are not daunted, and we are still as determined as ever to stand side by side with other cities who have felt the blast of the enemy, and we shall, with them, persevere with an unflagging spirit towards a conclusive and decisive victory.

— Sir Denis Daley, January 1941[[105]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHewitt2013146-108)

Portsmouth Harbour was a vital military embarkation point for the 6 June 1944 D-Day landings. [Southwick House](/wiki/Southwick_House "Southwick House"), just north of the city, was the headquarters of Supreme Allied Commander [Dwight D. Eisenhower](/wiki/Dwight_D._Eisenhower "Dwight D. Eisenhower").[[106]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHewitt2013155,_156-109)[[107]](#cite_note-110) A [V-1 flying bomb](/wiki/V-1_flying_bomb "V-1 flying bomb") hit Newcomen Road on 15 July 1944, killing 15 people.[[24]](#cite_note-history2-27)

### 1945 to present

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Portsmouth&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: 1945 to present")]

Much of the city's housing stock was damaged during the war. The wreckage was cleared in an attempt to improve housing quality after the war; before permanent accommodations could be built, Portsmouth City Council built [prefabs](/wiki/Prefabs_in_the_United_Kingdom "Prefabs in the United Kingdom") for those who had lost their homes. More than 700 prefab houses were constructed between 1945 and 1947, some over bomb sites.[[24]](#cite_note-history2-27) The first permanent houses were built away from the city centre, in new developments such as Paulsgrove and [Leigh Park](/wiki/Leigh_Park "Leigh Park");[[108]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHewitt2013160-111)[[109]](#cite_note-Leigh_Park_history-112) construction of council estates in Paulsgrove was completed in 1953. The first Leigh Park housing estates were completed in 1949, although construction in the area continued until 1974.[[24]](#cite_note-history2-27) Builders still occasionally find [unexploded bombs](/wiki/Unexploded_ordnance "Unexploded ordnance"), such as on the site of the destroyed Hippodrome Theatre in 1984.[[110]](#cite_note-113) Despite efforts by the city council to build new housing, a 1955 survey indicated that 7,000 houses in Portsmouth were unfit for human habitation. A controversial decision was made to replace a section of the central city, including Landport, Somerstown and Buckland, with council housing during the 1960s and early 1970s. The success of the project and the quality of its housing are debatable.[[24]](#cite_note-history2-27)

[![Her Majesty's Yacht Britannia is docked in Portsmouth Harbour for the 50th anniversary of the D-Day Landings in 1994. More modern Royal Navy ships are docked in behind her, and the masts of the HMS Victory can be seen in the far background.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/80/The_Royal_Yacht_Britannia_in_Portsmouth_-_geograph.org.uk_-_1702549.jpg/250px-The_Royal_Yacht_Britannia_in_Portsmouth_-_geograph.org.uk_-_1702549.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_Royal_Yacht_Britannia_in_Portsmouth_-_geograph.org.uk_-_1702549.jpg)

[Her Majesty's Yacht *Britannia*](/wiki/HMY_Britannia "HMY Britannia") in Portsmouth Harbour during the 50th anniversary of the D-Day Landings in 1994. The masts of [HMS *Victory*](/wiki/HMS_Victory "HMS Victory") can be seen in the background.

Portsmouth was affected by the decline of the British Empire in the second half of the 20th century. Shipbuilding jobs fell from 46 per cent of the workforce in 1951 to 14 per cent in 1966, drastically reducing manpower in the dockyard. The city council attempted to create new work; an industrial estate was built in Fratton in 1948, and others were built at Paulsgrove and Farlington during the 1950s and 1960s.[[24]](#cite_note-history2-27) Although traditional industries such as brewing and corset manufacturing disappeared during this time, electrical engineering became a major employer. Despite the cutbacks in traditional sectors, Portsmouth remained attractive to industry. [Zurich Insurance Group](/wiki/Zurich_Insurance_Group "Zurich Insurance Group") moved their UK headquarters to the city in 1968, and [IBM](/wiki/IBM "IBM") relocated their European headquarters in 1979.[[24]](#cite_note-history2-27) Portsmouth's population had dropped from about 200,000 to 177,142 by the end of the 1960s.[[111]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHewitt2013161-114) Defence Secretary [John Nott](/wiki/John_Nott "John Nott") decided in the early 1980s that of the four home dockyards, Portsmouth and [Chatham](/wiki/Chatham_Dockyard "Chatham Dockyard") would be closed. The city council won a concession, however, and the dockyard was downgraded instead to a naval base.[[112]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHewitt201392-115)

In 1956, the Soviet cruiser [Ordzhonikidze](/wiki/Indonesian_cruiser_Irian "Indonesian cruiser Irian") docked in Portsmouth harbour on a diplomatic mission that had taken head of state [Nikita Khrushchev](/wiki/Nikita_Khrushchev "Nikita Khrushchev") and [Nikolai Bulganin](/wiki/Nikolai_Bulganin "Nikolai Bulganin") to Britain. Naval intelligence was interested in the design of the ship and [MI6](/wiki/MI6 "MI6") recruited diver [Lionel Crabb](/wiki/Lionel_Crabb "Lionel Crabb") to collect intelligence on the ship particularly its propulsion. After diving into the harbour Crabb was never seen again. This led to a diplomatic incident with the Soviet Union and scandal in British domestic politics.[[113]](#cite_note-116)

On 2 April 1982, Argentine forces invaded two British territories in the South Atlantic: the [Falkland Islands](/wiki/Falkland_Islands "Falkland Islands") and [South Georgia and the South Sandwich Islands](/wiki/South_Georgia_and_the_South_Sandwich_Islands "South Georgia and the South Sandwich Islands"). The British government's response was to dispatch a [naval task force](/wiki/British_logistics_in_the_Falklands_War "British logistics in the Falklands War"), and the aircraft carriers [HMS *Hermes*](/wiki/HMS_Hermes_(R12) "HMS Hermes (R12)") and [HMS *Invincible*](/wiki/HMS_Invincible_(R05) "HMS Invincible (R05)") sailed from Portsmouth for the South Atlantic on 5 April. The successful outcome of the war reaffirmed Portsmouth's significance as a naval port and its importance to the defence of British interests.[[114]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHewitt201393-117) In January 1997, [Her Majesty's Yacht *Britannia*](/wiki/HMY_Britannia "HMY Britannia") embarked from the city on her final voyage to oversee the handover of Hong Kong; for many, this marked the end of the empire.[[115]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrendon2007660-118)[[116]](#cite_note-119) She was decommissioned on 11 December of that year at Portsmouth Naval Base in the presence of [Elizabeth II](/wiki/Elizabeth_II "Elizabeth II"), the [Duke of Edinburgh](/wiki/Prince_Philip,_Duke_of_Edinburgh "Prince Philip, Duke of Edinburgh"), and twelve senior members of the royal family.[[117]](#cite_note-120)[[118]](#cite_note-121)

Redevelopment of the naval shore establishment [HMS *Vernon*](/wiki/HMS_Vernon_(shore_establishment) "HMS Vernon (shore establishment)") began in 2001 as a complex of retail outlets, clubs, pubs, and a shopping centre known as Gunwharf Quays.[[24]](#cite_note-history2-27) Construction of the 552-foot-tall (168 m) [Spinnaker Tower](/wiki/Spinnaker_Tower "Spinnaker Tower"), sponsored by the [National Lottery](/wiki/National_Lottery_(United_Kingdom) "National Lottery (United Kingdom)"), began at Gunwharf Quays in 2003.[[119]](#cite_note-122) The [Tricorn Centre](/wiki/Tricorn_Centre "Tricorn Centre"), called "the ugliest building in the UK" by the BBC, was demolished in late 2004 after years of debate over the expense of demolition and whether it was worth preserving as an example of 1960s [brutalist architecture](/wiki/Brutalist_architecture "Brutalist architecture").[[120]](#cite_note-123)[[121]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEClark2009-124)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*] Designed by [Owen Luder](/wiki/Owen_Luder "Owen Luder") as part of a project to "revitalise" Portsmouth in the 1960s, it consisted of a shopping centre, market, nightclubs, and a [multistorey car park](/wiki/Multistorey_car_park "Multistorey car park").[[122]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHewitt2013164-125) Portsmouth celebrated the 200th anniversary of the [Battle of Trafalgar](/wiki/Battle_of_Trafalgar "Battle of Trafalgar") in 2005, with Queen Elizabeth II present at a [fleet review](/wiki/Fleet_review "Fleet review") and a mock battle.[[24]](#cite_note-history2-27) The naval base is home to two-thirds of Britain's surface fleet.[[123]](#cite_note-RN1-126) The city also hosted international commemorations for 50th, 75th and 80th anniversaries of the D-Day landings, these were attended by international leaders and remaining veterans.

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Portsmouth&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Geography")]

[![An aerial view of western side of Portsmouth (including Gunwharf Quays, the dockyard and the Spinnaker tower), the harbour itself, and the town of Gosport](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fc/Aerial_photograph_of_Portsmouth_Dockyard_taken_during_a_Photex%2C_taken_from_2%2C000_feet._MOD_45144955.jpg/250px-Aerial_photograph_of_Portsmouth_Dockyard_taken_during_a_Photex%2C_taken_from_2%2C000_feet._MOD_45144955.jpg)](/wiki/File:Aerial_photograph_of_Portsmouth_Dockyard_taken_during_a_Photex,_taken_from_2,000_feet._MOD_45144955.jpg)

Aerial view of Portsmouth and Portsmouth Harbour

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/86/London%2C_Great_Britain_Population_Density_and_Low_Elevation_Coastal_Zones_%285457306673%29.jpg/250px-London%2C_Great_Britain_Population_Density_and_Low_Elevation_Coastal_Zones_%285457306673%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:London,_Great_Britain_Population_Density_and_Low_Elevation_Coastal_Zones_(5457306673).jpg)

England population density and low elevation coastal zones. Portsmouth is particularly vulnerable to [sea level rise](/wiki/Sea_level_rise "Sea level rise").

Portsmouth is 73.5 miles (118.3 km) by road from central London, 49.5 miles (79.7 km) west of [Brighton](/wiki/Brighton "Brighton"), and 22.3 miles (35.9 km) east of [Southampton](/wiki/Southampton "Southampton").[[124]](#cite_note-GM-127) It is located primarily on [Portsea Island](/wiki/Portsea_Island "Portsea Island") and is the United Kingdom's only island city, although the city has expanded to the mainland.[[125]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHewitt201314,_16-128) [Gosport](/wiki/Gosport "Gosport") is a town and borough to the west.[[124]](#cite_note-GM-127) Portsea Island is separated from the mainland by [Portsbridge Creek](/wiki/Portsbridge_Creek "Portsbridge Creek"),[[126]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVine1990-129)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*] which is crossed by three road bridges (the [M275 motorway](/wiki/M275_motorway "M275 motorway"), the [A3 road](/wiki/A3_road "A3 road"), and the [A2030 road](/wiki/A2030_road "A2030 road")), a railway bridge, and two footbridges.[[127]](#cite_note-130) Portsea Island, part of the [Hampshire Basin](/wiki/Hampshire_Basin "Hampshire Basin"),[[128]](#cite_note-131) is low-lying; most of the island is less than 3 metres (9.8 ft) [above sea level](/wiki/Height_above_sea_level "Height above sea level").[[129]](#cite_note-rising-132)[[130]](#cite_note-133) The island's highest natural elevation is the Kingston Cross road junction, at 21 feet (6.4 m) above ordinary spring tide.[[131]](#cite_note-highpoint-134)

[Old Portsmouth](/wiki/Old_Portsmouth "Old Portsmouth"), the original town, is in the south-west part of the island and includes [Portsmouth Point](/wiki/Portsmouth_Point "Portsmouth Point") (nicknamed Spice Island).[[132]](#cite_note-135) The main channel entering Portsmouth Harbour, west of the island,[[126]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVine1990-129)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*] passes between Old Portsmouth and Gosport.[[124]](#cite_note-GM-127) Portsmouth Harbour has a series of lakes, including Fountain Lake (near the commercial port), Portchester Lake (south central), Paulsgrove Lake (north), Brick Kiln Lake and Tipner (east), and Bombketch and Spider Lakes (west). Further northwest, around Portchester, are Wicor, Cams, and Great Cams Lakes.[[124]](#cite_note-GM-127) The large tidal inlet of [Langstone Harbour](/wiki/Langstone_Harbour "Langstone Harbour") is east of the island. The [Farlington Marshes](/wiki/Farlington_Marshes "Farlington Marshes"), in the north off the coast of [Farlington](/wiki/Farlington,_Hampshire "Farlington, Hampshire"), is a 125 hectares (310 acres) [grazing marsh](/wiki/Grazing_marsh "Grazing marsh") and saline lagoon. One of the oldest [local reserves](/wiki/Local_nature_reserve "Local nature reserve") in the county, built from reclaimed land in 1771, it provides a habitat for migratory wildfowl and [waders](/wiki/Wader "Wader").[[133]](#cite_note-136)

[![A high aerial view of Portsea Island (the island which Portsmouth is situated on), and neighbouring Hayling Island](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/25/SelseyHaylingPortsea.JPG/250px-SelseyHaylingPortsea.JPG)](/wiki/File:SelseyHaylingPortsea.JPG)

Portsea Island and Hayling Island

South of Portsmouth are [Spithead](/wiki/Spithead "Spithead"), the [Solent](/wiki/Solent "Solent"), and the [Isle of Wight](/wiki/Isle_of_Wight "Isle of Wight"). Its southern coast was fortified by the [Round Tower](/wiki/Round_Tower_(Portsmouth) "Round Tower (Portsmouth)"), the [Square Tower](/wiki/Square_Tower "Square Tower"), Southsea Castle, [Lumps Fort](/wiki/Lumps_Fort "Lumps Fort") and [Fort Cumberland](/wiki/Fort_Cumberland_(England) "Fort Cumberland (England)").[[134]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPatterson1985-137)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*] Four sea forts were built in the Solent by [Lord Palmerston](/wiki/Henry_John_Temple,_3rd_Viscount_Palmerston "Henry John Temple, 3rd Viscount Palmerston"): [Spitbank Fort](/wiki/Spitbank_Fort "Spitbank Fort"), [St Helens Fort](/wiki/St_Helens_Fort "St Helens Fort"), [Horse Sand Fort](/wiki/Horse_Sand_Fort "Horse Sand Fort") and [No Man's Land Fort](/wiki/No_Man%27s_Land_Fort "No Man's Land Fort").

The resort of [Southsea](/wiki/Southsea "Southsea") is on the central southern shoreline of Portsea Island,[[135]](#cite_note-pissea-138) and [Eastney](/wiki/Eastney "Eastney") is east.[[136]](#cite_note-eastney-139) Eastney Lake covered nearly 170 acres (69 hectares) in 1626.[[137]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWebb198911-140) North of Eastney is the residential [Milton](/wiki/Milton,_Portsmouth "Milton, Portsmouth") and an area of reclaimed land known as Milton Common (formerly Milton Lake),[[124]](#cite_note-GM-127) a "flat scrubby land with a series of freshwater lakes".[[138]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELong2007188-141) Further north on the east coast is [Baffins](/wiki/Baffins "Baffins"), with the Great Salterns recreation ground and golf course around [Portsmouth College](/wiki/Portsmouth_College "Portsmouth College").[[124]](#cite_note-GM-127)

The [Hilsea Lines](/wiki/Hilsea_Lines "Hilsea Lines") are a series of defunct fortifications on the island's north coast, bordering Portsbridge Creek and the mainland.[[139]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHewitt201380-142)[[140]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMitchell1988-143)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*] [Portsdown Hill](/wiki/Portsdown_Hill "Portsdown Hill") dominates the skyline in the north, and contains several large [Palmerston Forts](/wiki/Palmerston_Forts,_Portsmouth "Palmerston Forts, Portsmouth")[[d]](#cite_note-144) such as [Fort Fareham](/wiki/Fort_Fareham "Fort Fareham"), [Fort Wallington](/wiki/Fort_Wallington "Fort Wallington"), [Fort Nelson](/wiki/Fort_Nelson,_Hampshire "Fort Nelson, Hampshire"), [Fort Southwick](/wiki/Fort_Southwick "Fort Southwick"), [Fort Widley](/wiki/Fort_Widley "Fort Widley"), and [Fort Purbrook](/wiki/Fort_Purbrook "Fort Purbrook").[[134]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPatterson1985-137)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*][[141]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHewitt201379,_80-145) Portsdown Hill is a large band of [chalk](/wiki/Chalk "Chalk"); the rest of Portsea Island is composed of layers of [London Clay](/wiki/London_Clay "London Clay") and [sand](/wiki/Sand "Sand") (part of the [Bagshot Formation](/wiki/Bagshot_Formation "Bagshot Formation")), formed principally during the [Eocene](/wiki/Eocene "Eocene").[[142]](#cite_note-146)

Northern areas of the city include [Stamshaw](/wiki/Stamshaw "Stamshaw"), [Hilsea](/wiki/Hilsea "Hilsea") and [Copnor](/wiki/Copnor "Copnor"), [Cosham](/wiki/Cosham "Cosham"), [Drayton](/wiki/Drayton,_Hampshire "Drayton, Hampshire"), [Farlington](/wiki/Farlington,_Hampshire "Farlington, Hampshire"), Paulsgrove and [Port Solent](/wiki/Port_Solent "Port Solent").[[143]](#cite_note-147) Other districts include [North End](/wiki/North_End,_Hampshire "North End, Hampshire") and Fratton.[[144]](#cite_note-148)[[145]](#cite_note-149) The west of the city contains [council estates](/wiki/Public_housing_in_the_United_Kingdom "Public housing in the United Kingdom"), such as [Buckland](/wiki/Buckland,_Portsmouth "Buckland, Portsmouth"), [Landport](/wiki/Landport "Landport"), and Portsea, which replaced Victorian terraces destroyed by Second World War bombing.[[24]](#cite_note-history2-27) After the war, the 2,000-acre (810 ha) [Leigh Park](/wiki/Leigh_Park "Leigh Park") estate was built to address the chronic housing shortage during post-war reconstruction.[[108]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHewitt2013160-111) Although the estate has been under the jurisdiction of [Havant Borough Council](/wiki/Borough_of_Havant "Borough of Havant") since the early 2000s, Portsmouth City Council remains its landlord (the borough's largest landowner).[[109]](#cite_note-Leigh_Park_history-112)

The city's main station, [Portsmouth and Southsea railway station](/wiki/Portsmouth_and_Southsea_railway_station "Portsmouth and Southsea railway station"),[[146]](#cite_note-150) is in the city centre near the [Guildhall](/wiki/Portsmouth_Guildhall "Portsmouth Guildhall") and the [Civic Offices](/wiki/Portsmouth_Civic_Offices "Portsmouth Civic Offices").[[99]](#cite_note-guild-102)[[147]](#cite_note-151) South of the Guildhall is Guildhall Walk, with a number of pubs and clubs.[[148]](#cite_note-152) The city's other railway station, [Portsmouth Harbour railway station](/wiki/Portsmouth_Harbour_railway_station "Portsmouth Harbour railway station"), is located on a pier at the harbour's edge, near Old Portsmouth.[[149]](#cite_note-153) Edinburgh Road contains the city's Roman Catholic cathedral and [Victoria Park](/wiki/Victoria_Park,_Portsmouth "Victoria Park, Portsmouth"), a 15-acre (6.1 ha) park which opened in 1878.[[150]](#cite_note-vic-154)

Langstone Harbour and Hayling Island are on the left, and Portsmouth Harbour is on the right.")

### Climate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Portsmouth&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Climate")]

Portsmouth has a mild [oceanic climate](/wiki/Oceanic_climate "Oceanic climate"), with more sunshine than most of the British Isles.[[151]](#cite_note-155) Frosts are light and short-lived and snow is quite rare in winter, with temperatures rarely dropping below freezing.[[129]](#cite_note-rising-132) The average maximum temperature in January is 10 °C (50 °F), and the average minimum is 5 °C (41 °F). The lowest recorded temperature is −8 °C (18 °F).[[152]](#cite_note-metextreme-156) In summer, temperatures sometimes reach 30 °C (86 °F). The average maximum temperature in July is 22 °C (72 °F), and the average minimum is 15 °C (59 °F). The highest recorded temperature is 35 °C (95 °F).[[152]](#cite_note-metextreme-156) The city gets about 645 millimetres (25.4 in) of rain annually, with a minimum of 1 mm (0.04 in) of rain reported 103 days per year.[[153]](#cite_note-weather-157)

## Demography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Portsmouth&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Demography")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9d/Portsmouth_population_pyramid.svg/250px-Portsmouth_population_pyramid.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Portsmouth_population_pyramid.svg)

Population pyramid of Portsmouth (unitary authority) in 2021

Portsmouth is the second-most densely populated city in the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom"), after [London](/wiki/London "London").[[156]](#cite_note-PortsmouthCouncil-161)[[157]](#cite_note-OffNatStat-162) In the [2021 census](/wiki/2021_United_Kingdom_census "2021 United Kingdom census"), the city had 208,100 residents.[[6]](#cite_note-auto-9) The city used to be even more densely populated, with the 1951 census showing a population of 233,545.[[158]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDickinson1998390-163)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*][[159]](#cite_note-164) In a reversal of that decrease, its population has been gradually increasing since the 1990s.[[160]](#cite_note-165) With about 860,000 residents, [South Hampshire](/wiki/South_Hampshire "South Hampshire") is the [fifth-largest urban area in England](/wiki/List_of_urban_areas_in_the_United_Kingdom "List of urban areas in the United Kingdom") and the largest in [South-East England](/wiki/South-East_England "South-East England") outside London; it is the centre of one of the United Kingdom's most-populous metropolitan areas.[[161]](#cite_note-166)

The city is predominantly white (85.3% of the population). However, Portsmouth's long association with the Royal Navy ensures some diversity.[[162]](#cite_note-hant-167) Some large, well-established non-white communities have their roots in the Royal Navy, particularly the [Chinese](/wiki/Chinese_people "Chinese people") community from [British Hong Kong](/wiki/British_Hong_Kong "British Hong Kong").[[162]](#cite_note-hant-167)[[163]](#cite_note-168) Portsmouth's long industrial history with the Royal Navy has drawn many people from across the British Isles (particularly Irish Catholics) to its factories and docks.[[164]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDaly201127-169)[[f]](#cite_note-172) According to the 2021 census, Portsmouth's population was 78% [White British](/wiki/White_British "White British"), 6.8% [other White](/wiki/Other_White "Other White"), 2.3% [Bangladeshi](/wiki/British_Bangladeshi "British Bangladeshi"), 2.6% [mixed race](/wiki/Mixed_(United_Kingdom_ethnicity_category) "Mixed (United Kingdom ethnicity category)"), 1.5% [Indian](/wiki/British_Indians "British Indians"), 1.0% [Chinese](/wiki/British_Chinese "British Chinese"), 1.8% [other](/wiki/2001_United_Kingdom_census "2001 United Kingdom census"), 0.5% [Black African](/wiki/Black_British_people "Black British people"), 0.5% [white Irish](/wiki/Irish_migration_to_Great_Britain "Irish migration to Great Britain"), 1.8% [other Asian](/wiki/British_Asian "British Asian"), 0.3% [Pakistani](/wiki/British_Pakistanis "British Pakistanis"), 2.6% [Black Caribbean](/wiki/British_African-Caribbean_people "British African-Caribbean people") and 0.4% other Black.[[167]](#cite_note-:0-173)
