## Politics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Portsmouth&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Politics")]

[![A front-facing view of Portsmouth Guildhall and the surrounding civic offices](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/20/Portsmouth_Guildhall_2014.JPG/250px-Portsmouth_Guildhall_2014.JPG)](/wiki/File:Portsmouth_Guildhall_2014.JPG)

The neo-classical [Portsmouth Guildhall](/wiki/Portsmouth_Guildhall "Portsmouth Guildhall") and surrounding [Civic Offices](/wiki/Portsmouth_Civic_Offices "Portsmouth Civic Offices") are the centre of government.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4a/PortsmouthNorth2007Constituency.svg/250px-PortsmouthNorth2007Constituency.svg.png)](/wiki/File:PortsmouthNorth2007Constituency.svg)

[Portsmouth North](/wiki/Portsmouth_North_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Portsmouth North (UK Parliament constituency)")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5d/PortsmouthSouth2007Constituency.svg/250px-PortsmouthSouth2007Constituency.svg.png)](/wiki/File:PortsmouthSouth2007Constituency.svg)

[Portsmouth South](/wiki/Portsmouth_South_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Portsmouth South (UK Parliament constituency)")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5d/Portsmouth_UK_ward_map_2010_%28blank%29.svg/250px-Portsmouth_UK_ward_map_2010_%28blank%29.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Portsmouth_UK_ward_map_2010_(blank).svg)

The 14 electoral wards of Portsmouth

The city is administered by [Portsmouth City Council](/wiki/Portsmouth_City_Council "Portsmouth City Council"), a [unitary authority](/wiki/Unitary_authority "Unitary authority") which is responsible for local affairs. Portsmouth was granted its first market town charter in 1194.[[173]](#cite_note-179) In 1904, its boundaries were extended to all of Portsea Island and were later expanded onto the mainland of [Great Britain](/wiki/Great_Britain "Great Britain") between 1920 and 1932 by incorporating Paulsgrove, [Wymering](/wiki/Wymering "Wymering"), [Cosham](/wiki/Cosham "Cosham"), [Drayton](/wiki/Drayton,_Hampshire "Drayton, Hampshire") and [Farlington](/wiki/Farlington,_Hampshire "Farlington, Hampshire") into Portsmouth.[[174]](#cite_note-portsmouth1-180) Portsmouth was granted [city status](/wiki/City_status_in_the_United_Kingdom "City status in the United Kingdom") on 21 April 1926.[[174]](#cite_note-portsmouth1-180)

On 1 April 1974, it formed the second tier of local government (below [Hampshire County Council](/wiki/Hampshire_County_Council "Hampshire County Council"));[[175]](#cite_note-181) Portsmouth and Southampton became administratively independent of Hampshire with the creation of the unitary authority on 1 April 1997.[[176]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHewitt201318-182)

The city is divided into two parliamentary constituencies, [Portsmouth South](/wiki/Portsmouth_South_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Portsmouth South (UK Parliament constituency)") and [Portsmouth North](/wiki/Portsmouth_North_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Portsmouth North (UK Parliament constituency)"), represented in the [House of Commons](/wiki/British_House_of_Commons "British House of Commons") by [Stephen Morgan](/wiki/Stephen_Morgan_(U.K._politician) "Stephen Morgan (U.K. politician)") and [Amanda Martin](/wiki/Amanda_Martin "Amanda Martin"), both of the [Labour Party](/wiki/Labour_Party_(UK) "Labour Party (UK)").[[177]](#cite_note-183) The two Parliamentary constituencies each contain 7 electoral wards, giving an overall total of 14 electoral wards. Portsmouth's inner city centre is located in the Portsmouth South constituency.

Portsmouth City Council has 14 electoral wards, each ward returns three councillors, making 42 in total.[[178]](#cite_note-ward-184) Each councillor serves a four-year term.[[179]](#cite_note-185) After the [May 2018 local elections](/wiki/2018_United_Kingdom_local_elections "2018 United Kingdom local elections"), the [Liberal Democrats](/wiki/Liberal_Democrats_(UK) "Liberal Democrats (UK)") formed a minority administration, they have run the city since then. The leader of the council is the Liberal Democrat, [Gerald Vernon-Jackson](/wiki/Gerald_Vernon-Jackson "Gerald Vernon-Jackson"). The lord mayor usually has a one-year term.[[180]](#cite_note-186)

The council is based in the [Civic Offices](/wiki/Portsmouth_Civic_Offices "Portsmouth Civic Offices"), which house the tax support, housing-benefits, resident-services, and municipal-functions departments.[[181]](#cite_note-187) They are in Guildhall Square, with the [Portsmouth Guildhall](/wiki/Portsmouth_Guildhall "Portsmouth Guildhall") and Portsmouth Central Library. The Guildhall, a symbol of Portsmouth, is a cultural venue. It was designed by [Leeds](/wiki/Leeds "Leeds")-based architect [William Hill](/wiki/William_Hill_(English_architect) "William Hill (English architect)"), who began it in the [neo-classical style](/wiki/Neoclassicism "Neoclassicism") in 1873 at a cost of £140,000.[[105]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHewitt2013146-108)[[182]](#cite_note-188) It was opened to the public in 1890.[[183]](#cite_note-189)

### Minister for Portsmouth

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Portsmouth&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Minister for Portsmouth")]

Between January 2014 and July 2016, Portsmouth uniquely had a dedicated government minister, the [Minister for Portsmouth](/wiki/Minister_for_Portsmouth "Minister for Portsmouth"), a position created in response to the loss of 900 jobs from BAE Systems within HMNB Portsmouth. The minister was charged with bringing economic growth to the city.[[184]](#cite_note-190)

## Economy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Portsmouth&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Economy")]

[![In this photograph, many large containers and other cargo are lined up in the city's ferry port. A ferry can be seen docked in the background.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b5/Vehicles_lining_up_to_embark_at_the_continental_ferry_port%2C_Portsmouth_-_geograph.org.uk_-_499738.jpg/250px-Vehicles_lining_up_to_embark_at_the_continental_ferry_port%2C_Portsmouth_-_geograph.org.uk_-_499738.jpg)](/wiki/File:Vehicles_lining_up_to_embark_at_the_continental_ferry_port,_Portsmouth_-_geograph.org.uk_-_499738.jpg)

[Portsmouth International Port](/wiki/Portsmouth_International_Port "Portsmouth International Port") is a major employer.

Ten per cent of Portsmouth's workforce is employed at [Portsmouth Naval Dockyard](/wiki/HMNB_Portsmouth "HMNB Portsmouth"), which is linked to the city's biggest industry, defence; the headquarters of [BAE Systems Surface Ships](/wiki/BAE_Systems_Surface_Ships "BAE Systems Surface Ships") is in the city.[[185]](#cite_note-191) BAE's Portsmouth shipyard received construction work on the two new [*Queen Elizabeth*-class aircraft carriers](/wiki/Queen_Elizabeth-class_aircraft_carrier "Queen Elizabeth-class aircraft carrier").[[186]](#cite_note-192)[[187]](#cite_note-193)[[188]](#cite_note-tel-194) A £100 million contract was signed to develop needed facilities for the vessels.[[188]](#cite_note-tel-194) However defence shipbuilding was ended in the city in favour of Glasgow during the 2014 Scottish Independence referendum. The Government was accused of making the decision to keep the more antiquated Glasgow shipyard over Portsmouth for political reasons as part of the pro union campaign. Ministers at the time did state shipbuilding would return to the city if Scotland left the UK. A minister for Portsmouth was established to help deal with the economic fallout of the decision.[[189]](#cite_note-195)
A ferry port handles passengers and cargo,[[190]](#cite_note-196) and a fishing fleet of 20 to 30 boats operates out of Camber Quay, [Old Portsmouth](/wiki/Old_Portsmouth "Old Portsmouth"); most of the catch is sold at the quayside fish market.[[191]](#cite_note-fish-197)

The city is host to [IBM](/wiki/IBM "IBM")'s UK headquarters and Portsmouth was also the UK headquarters of [Zurich Financial Services](/wiki/Zurich_Financial_Services "Zurich Financial Services") until 2007.[[24]](#cite_note-history2-27)[[192]](#cite_note-198) City shopping is centred on Commercial Road and the 1980s Cascades Shopping Centre.[[193]](#cite_note-199)[[194]](#cite_note-200) The shopping centre has 185,000 to 230,000 visitors weekly.[[195]](#cite_note-201) Redevelopment has created new shopping areas, including the [Gunwharf Quays](/wiki/Gunwharf_Quays "Gunwharf Quays") (the repurposed [HMS *Vernon*](/wiki/HMS_Vernon_(shore_establishment) "HMS Vernon (shore establishment)") shore establishment,[[196]](#cite_note-guns-202) with stores, restaurants and a cinema) and the Historic Dockyard, which caters to tourists and holds an annual Victorian [Christmas market](/wiki/Christmas_market "Christmas market").[[197]](#cite_note-203)[[198]](#cite_note-gun-204) Ocean Retail Park, on the north-eastern side of Portsea Island, was built in September 1985 on the site of a former metal-box factory.[[199]](#cite_note-205)

[![A view of some shops in the Gunwharf Quays shopping centre.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/96/Gunwharf_Quays_Vernon_Avenue.JPG/250px-Gunwharf_Quays_Vernon_Avenue.JPG)](/wiki/File:Gunwharf_Quays_Vernon_Avenue.JPG)

[Gunwharf Quays](/wiki/Gunwharf_Quays "Gunwharf Quays") shopping centre

Development of Gunwharf Quays continued until 2007, when the 330-foot-tall (101 m) No. 1 Gunwharf Quays residential tower was completed.[[200]](#cite_note-206)[[201]](#cite_note-207) The development of the former Brickwoods Brewery site included the construction of the 22-storey Admiralty Quarter Tower, the tallest in a complex of primarily low-rise residential buildings.[[202]](#cite_note-208) Number One Portsmouth, a proposed 25-storey 330 feet (101 m) tower opposite Portsmouth & Southsea station, was announced at the end of October 2008.[[203]](#cite_note-209) In August 2009, internal demolition of the existing building had begun.[[204]](#cite_note-210) A high-rise student dormitory, nicknamed "The Blade", has begun construction on the site of the [swimming baths](/wiki/Swimming_pool "Swimming pool") at the edge of Victoria Park. The 300-foot (91 m) tower will be Portsmouth's second-tallest structure, after the Spinnaker Tower.[[205]](#cite_note-211)

In April 2007, Portsmouth F.C. announced plans to move from Fratton Park to a new stadium on reclaimed land next to the Historic Dockyard. The £600 million mixed-use development, designed by [Herzog & de Meuron](/wiki/Herzog_%26_de_Meuron "Herzog & de Meuron"), would include shops, offices and 1,500 harbourside apartments.[[206]](#cite_note-SAA-212)[[207]](#cite_note-213) The scheme was criticised for its size and location, and some officials said that it would interfere with harbour operations.[[208]](#cite_note-214)[[209]](#cite_note-215) The project was rejected by the city council due to the [2008 financial crisis](/wiki/2008_financial_crisis "2008 financial crisis").[[210]](#cite_note-216)

[![A Queen Elizabeth-class aircraft carrier docked in Scotland. This ship is one of two aircraft carriers, Portsmouth is its home port.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/00/HMS_Queen_Elizabeth_in_Rosyth_Dockyard_MOD_45158229.jpg/250px-HMS_Queen_Elizabeth_in_Rosyth_Dockyard_MOD_45158229.jpg)](/wiki/File:HMS_Queen_Elizabeth_in_Rosyth_Dockyard_MOD_45158229.jpg)

Portsmouth is the home port of the two *Queen Elizabeth*-class aircraft carriers.

Portsmouth's two Queen Elizabeth-class aircraft carriers, [HMS *Queen Elizabeth*](/wiki/HMS_Queen_Elizabeth_(R08) "HMS Queen Elizabeth (R08)") and [HMS *Prince of Wales*](/wiki/HMS_Prince_of_Wales_(R09) "HMS Prince of Wales (R09)"), were ordered by defence secretary [Des Browne](/wiki/Des_Browne "Des Browne") on 25 July 2007.[[211]](#cite_note-217) They were built in the Firth of Forth at [Rosyth Dockyard](/wiki/Rosyth_Dockyard "Rosyth Dockyard") and [BAE Systems Surface Ships](/wiki/BAE_Systems_Surface_Ships "BAE Systems Surface Ships") in Glasgow, [Babcock International](/wiki/Babcock_International "Babcock International") at Rosyth, and at HMNB Portsmouth.[[212]](#cite_note-218)[[213]](#cite_note-219) The government announced before the [2014 Scottish independence referendum](/wiki/2014_Scottish_independence_referendum "2014 Scottish independence referendum") that military shipbuilding would end in Portsmouth, with all UK surface-warship construction focused on the two older BAE facilities in Glasgow.[[214]](#cite_note-end-220) The announcement was criticised by local politicians as a political decision to aid the referendum's "No" campaign.[[215]](#cite_note-221)

## Culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Portsmouth&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Culture")]

Portsmouth has several theatres. The [New Theatre Royal](/wiki/New_Theatre_Royal "New Theatre Royal") in Guildhall Walk, near the city centre, specialises in professional drama.[[216]](#cite_note-222) The restored Kings Theatre in Southsea features amateur musicals and national tours.[[217]](#cite_note-223) The Groundlings Theatre, built in 1784, is housed at the Old Beneficial School in Portsea.[[218]](#cite_note-224) New Prince's Theatre and Southsea's Kings Theatre were designed by Victorian architect [Frank Matcham](/wiki/Frank_Matcham "Frank Matcham").[[219]](#cite_note-225)

The city has three musical venues: the Guildhall,[[220]](#cite_note-226) [the Wedgewood Rooms](/wiki/The_Wedgewood_Rooms "The Wedgewood Rooms") (which includes Edge of the Wedge, a smaller venue),[[221]](#cite_note-227) and [Portsmouth Pyramids Centre](/wiki/Portsmouth_Pyramids_Centre "Portsmouth Pyramids Centre").[[222]](#cite_note-228) [Portsmouth Guildhall](/wiki/Portsmouth_Guildhall "Portsmouth Guildhall") is one of the largest venues in [South East England](/wiki/South_East_England "South East England"), with a [seating capacity](/wiki/Seating_capacity "Seating capacity") of 2,500.[[99]](#cite_note-guild-102)[[223]](#cite_note-229)[[224]](#cite_note-230) A concert series is presented at the Guildhall by the [Bournemouth Symphony Orchestra](/wiki/Bournemouth_Symphony_Orchestra "Bournemouth Symphony Orchestra").[[225]](#cite_note-231) The [Portsmouth Sinfonia](/wiki/Portsmouth_Sinfonia "Portsmouth Sinfonia") approached classical music from a different angle during the 1970s, recruiting players with [no musical training](/wiki/Outsider_music "Outsider music") or who played an instrument new to them.[[226]](#cite_note-232)[[227]](#cite_note-233) The Portsmouth Summer Show is held at [King George's Fields](/wiki/King_George%27s_Fields "King George's Fields"). The 2016 show held during the last weekend of April, featured [cover bands](/wiki/Cover_band "Cover band") such as the Silver Beatles, the Bog Rolling Stones, and Fleetingwood Mac.[[228]](#cite_note-234)

A number of musical works are set in the city. *[H.M.S. Pinafore](/wiki/H.M.S._Pinafore "H.M.S. Pinafore")* is a comic opera in two acts set in Portsmouth Harbour, with music by [Arthur Sullivan](/wiki/Arthur_Sullivan "Arthur Sullivan") and libretto by [W.S. Gilbert](/wiki/W.S._Gilbert "W.S. Gilbert").[[229]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStedman1996157–158-235) [*Portsmouth Point*](/wiki/Portsmouth_Point_(Walton) "Portsmouth Point (Walton)") is a 1925 [overture](/wiki/Overture "Overture") for orchestra by English composer [William Walton](/wiki/William_Walton "William Walton"), inspired by [Thomas Rowlandson](/wiki/Thomas_Rowlandson "Thomas Rowlandson")'s etching of Portsmouth Point in Old Portsmouth.[[230]](#cite_note-236)[[231]](#cite_note-237) The overture has been recorded many times,[[232]](#cite_note-238) and often played at the BBC [Proms](/wiki/The_Proms "The Proms").[[233]](#cite_note-239) [John Cranko](/wiki/John_Cranko "John Cranko")'s 1951 ballet *[Pineapple Poll](/wiki/Pineapple_Poll "Pineapple Poll")*, which features music from [Gilbert and Sullivan](/wiki/Gilbert_and_Sullivan "Gilbert and Sullivan")'s operetta *The Bumboat Woman's Story*, is also set in Portsmouth.[[234]](#cite_note-240)[[235]](#cite_note-241)

Portsmouth hosts yearly remembrances of the D-Day landings, attended by veterans from Allied and Commonwealth nations.[[236]](#cite_note-242)[[237]](#cite_note-243) The city played a major role in the 50th D-Day anniversary in 1994; visitors included US President [Bill Clinton](/wiki/Bill_Clinton "Bill Clinton"), Australian Prime Minister [Paul Keating](/wiki/Paul_Keating "Paul Keating"), King [Harald V of Norway](/wiki/Harald_V_of_Norway "Harald V of Norway"), French President [François Mitterrand](/wiki/Fran%C3%A7ois_Mitterrand "François Mitterrand"), New Zealand Prime Minister [Jim Bolger](/wiki/Jim_Bolger "Jim Bolger"), Canadian Prime Minister [Jean Chrétien](/wiki/Jean_Chr%C3%A9tien "Jean Chrétien"), Prime Minister [John Major](/wiki/John_Major "John Major"), the Queen, and the Duke of Edinburgh.[[238]](#cite_note-244)[[239]](#cite_note-245) The 75th Anniversary of D-Day was similarly commemorated in the city. Prime Minister [Theresa May](/wiki/Theresa_May "Theresa May") led the event, and was joined by leaders of the US, Canada, Australia, France and Germany.[[240]](#cite_note-Mohan-Hickson-246)

The annual Portsmouth International Kite Festival, organised by the city council and the Kite Society of Great Britain, celebrated its 25th anniversary in 2016.[[241]](#cite_note-247)

[Victorious Festival](/wiki/Victorious_Festival "Victorious Festival"), the biggest metropolitan music festival in the UK takes place on Southsea Seafront in Portsmouth. It has been an annual event at this location since 2014. It is a large family-friendly music festival and has featured headliners including [Stereophonics](/wiki/Stereophonics "Stereophonics"), [Noel Gallagher's High Flying Birds](/wiki/Noel_Gallagher%27s_High_Flying_Birds "Noel Gallagher's High Flying Birds"), [The Prodigy](/wiki/The_Prodigy "The Prodigy"), and other prominent household names.[[242]](#cite_note-charity-248)

Portsmouth is frequently used as a filming location for television and film productions, especially the Historic Dockyard. Productions include [*Tommy*](/wiki/Tommy_(1975_film) "Tommy (1975 film)"), *[Tomorrow Never Dies](/wiki/Tomorrow_Never_Dies "Tomorrow Never Dies")* (1997), [*Mansfield Park*](/wiki/Mansfield_Park_(1999_film) "Mansfield Park (1999 film)") and the Hollywood adaptation of *[Les Miserables](/wiki/Les_Miserables "Les Miserables")*.[[243]](#cite_note-249)[[244]](#cite_note-250)

In 2005, Portsmouth featured in the first series of ITV's *Britain's Toughest Towns*.[[245]](#cite_note-251) As this documentary also indicated, Portsmouth has issues with gangs and anti-social behaviour.[[246]](#cite_note-News_May_2021-252)[[247]](#cite_note-News_June_2021...-253)[[248]](#cite_note-News_June_2021-254)[[249]](#cite_note-HL_June_2020-255)[[250]](#cite_note-News_Sept_2020-256)

Portsmouth is home to Paul Stone, the street artist known as [My Dog Sighs](/wiki/My_Dog_Sighs "My Dog Sighs").[[251]](#cite_note-257) In 2021, for his INSIDE exhibition, he transformed a derelict ballroom into an immersive world inhabited by creatures that he called 'Quiet Little Voices'.[[252]](#cite_note-258) In 2023 he celebrated 20 years as a street artist by hiding £30,000 of art across the city in a [Free Art](/wiki/Gifted_art "Gifted art") giveaway.[[253]](#cite_note-259) He has been awarded an honorary doctorate by [University of Portsmouth](/wiki/University_of_Portsmouth "University of Portsmouth").[[254]](#cite_note-260)

Portsmouth City Council made a bid on 28 November 2025 to become [UK City of Culture](/wiki/UK_City_of_Culture "UK City of Culture") 2029.[[255]](#cite_note-261)

## Literature

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Portsmouth&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Literature")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e3/Statue_of_Charles_Dickens_in_Guildhall_Square_-_geograph.org.uk_-_3871763.jpg/250px-Statue_of_Charles_Dickens_in_Guildhall_Square_-_geograph.org.uk_-_3871763.jpg)](/wiki/File:Statue_of_Charles_Dickens_in_Guildhall_Square_-_geograph.org.uk_-_3871763.jpg)

This statue to [Charles Dickens](/wiki/Charles_Dickens "Charles Dickens") in Portsmouth is one of only three statues to the historic writer in the world. Dickens wrote in his will that he did not want such statues built in his honour.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Portsmouth is the hometown of Fanny Price, the main character of [Jane Austen](/wiki/Jane_Austen "Jane Austen")'s novel *[Mansfield Park](/wiki/Mansfield_Park "Mansfield Park")*, and most of its closing chapters are set there.[[256]](#cite_note-262) Nicholas and Smike, the main protagonists of [Charles Dickens](/wiki/Charles_Dickens "Charles Dickens")' novel *[The Life and Adventures of Nicholas Nickleby](/wiki/The_Life_and_Adventures_of_Nicholas_Nickleby "The Life and Adventures of Nicholas Nickleby")*, make their way to Portsmouth and become involved with a theatrical troupe.[[257]](#cite_note-263) Portsmouth is most often the port from which Captain Jack Aubrey's ships sail in [Patrick O'Brian](/wiki/Patrick_O%27Brian "Patrick O'Brian")'s seafaring historical [Aubrey-Maturin series](/wiki/Aubrey-Maturin_series "Aubrey-Maturin series").[[258]](#cite_note-264) Portsmouth is the main setting of [Jonathan Meades](/wiki/Jonathan_Meades "Jonathan Meades")'s 1993 novel *Pompey*.[[259]](#cite_note-265) Since the novel was published, Meades has presented a TV programme documenting Victorian architecture in Portsmouth Dockyard.[[260]](#cite_note-266)

Victorian novelist and historian [Sir Walter Besant](/wiki/Sir_Walter_Besant "Sir Walter Besant") documented his 1840s childhood in *By Celia's Arbour: A Tale of Portsmouth Town*, precisely describing the town before its defensive walls were removed.[[261]](#cite_note-267) Southsea (as Port Burdock) features in *[The History of Mr Polly](/wiki/The_History_of_Mr_Polly "The History of Mr Polly")* by [H. G. Wells](/wiki/H._G._Wells "H. G. Wells"), who describes it as "one of the three townships that are grouped around the Port Burdock naval dockyards".[[262]](#cite_note-268) The resort is also the setting of the [graphic novel](/wiki/Graphic_novel "Graphic novel") *[The Tragical Comedy or Comical Tragedy of Mr. Punch](/wiki/The_Tragical_Comedy_or_Comical_Tragedy_of_Mr._Punch "The Tragical Comedy or Comical Tragedy of Mr. Punch")* by [high fantasy](/wiki/High_fantasy "High fantasy") author [Neil Gaiman](/wiki/Neil_Gaiman "Neil Gaiman"), who grew up in Portsmouth. A Southsea street was renamed The Ocean at the End of the Lane by the city council in honour of Gaiman's [novel of the same name](/wiki/The_Ocean_at_the_End_of_the_Lane "The Ocean at the End of the Lane").[[263]](#cite_note-Flood-269)[[264]](#cite_note-270)

Crime novels set in Portsmouth and the surrounding area include [Graham Hurley](/wiki/Graham_Hurley "Graham Hurley")'s D.I. Faraday/D.C. Winter novels[[265]](#cite_note-graham-271) and [C. J. Sansom](/wiki/C._J._Sansom "C. J. Sansom")'s Tudor crime novel, *Heartstone*; the latter refers to the warship *Mary Rose* and describes Tudor life in the town.[[266]](#cite_note-272) *Portsmouth Fairy Tales for Grown Ups*, a collection of short stories, was published in 2014.[[267]](#cite_note-273)[[268]](#cite_note-274) The collection, set around Portsmouth, includes stories by crime novelists William Sutton and Diana Bretherick.[[269]](#cite_note-275)[[270]](#cite_note-276)

## Education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Portsmouth&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Education")]

See also: [List of schools in Portsmouth](/wiki/List_of_schools_in_Portsmouth "List of schools in Portsmouth")

[![A side-facing view of the Park Building, one of the buildings which make up the University of Portsmouth](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/27/Park_Building%2C_University_of_Portsmouth_-_geograph.org.uk_-_548659.jpg/250px-Park_Building%2C_University_of_Portsmouth_-_geograph.org.uk_-_548659.jpg)](/wiki/File:Park_Building,_University_of_Portsmouth_-_geograph.org.uk_-_548659.jpg)

Park Building, University of Portsmouth

The [University of Portsmouth](/wiki/University_of_Portsmouth "University of Portsmouth") was founded in 1992 as a [new university](/wiki/New_university "New university") from Portsmouth Polytechnic; in 2016, it had 20,000 students.[[271]](#cite_note-277) The university was ranked among the world's top 100 modern universities in April 2015.[[272]](#cite_note-Gurney-278)[[273]](#cite_note-uopnews-279) In 2013, it had about 23,000 students and over 2,500 staff members.[[274]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHewitt2013198-280) Several local colleges also award [Higher National Diplomas](/wiki/Higher_National_Diploma "Higher National Diploma"), including [Highbury College](/wiki/Highbury_College "Highbury College") (specialising in vocational education),[[275]](#cite_note-281) and Portsmouth College (which offers academic courses).[[276]](#cite_note-282) [Admiral Lord Nelson School](/wiki/Admiral_Lord_Nelson_School "Admiral Lord Nelson School") and [Miltoncross Academy](/wiki/Miltoncross_Academy "Miltoncross Academy") were built in the late 1990s to meet the needs of a growing school-age population.[[277]](#cite_note-283)[[278]](#cite_note-284)

After the cancellation of the national building programme for schools, redevelopment halted.[[279]](#cite_note-285) Two schools in the city were judged "inadequate", and 29 of its 63 schools were considered "no longer good enough" by [Ofsted](/wiki/Ofsted "Ofsted") in 2009.[[280]](#cite_note-286) Before it was taken over by [Ark Schools](/wiki/Ark_(charity) "Ark (charity)") and became [Ark Charter Academy](/wiki/Ark_Charter_Academy "Ark Charter Academy"), St Luke's Church of England secondary school was one of England's worst schools in GCSE achievement. It was criticised by officials for its behavioural standards, with students reportedly throwing chairs at teachers.[[281]](#cite_note-287) Since it became an academy in 2009, the school has improved; 69 per cent of its students achieved five GCSEs with grades of A\* to C, including English and mathematics.[[282]](#cite_note-288) The academy's intake policy is for a standard comprehensive school, drawing from the community rather than by religion.[[283]](#cite_note-289)

[Portsmouth Grammar School](/wiki/Portsmouth_Grammar_School "Portsmouth Grammar School"), the city's oldest independent school, was founded in 1732.[[284]](#cite_note-290)[[285]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHewitt2013200-291) Other independent schools include [Portsmouth High School](/wiki/Portsmouth_High_School,_Southsea "Portsmouth High School, Southsea"),[[286]](#cite_note-292) and [Mayville High School](/wiki/Mayville_High_School,_Southsea "Mayville High School, Southsea") (founded in 1897).[[287]](#cite_note-293)

## Landmarks

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Portsmouth&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Landmarks")]

[![A view of the port side of HMS Warrior alongside Portsmouth Harbour. The Spinnaker Tower can be seen to the far left.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0d/HMS_Warrior_and_Spinnaker_Tower.jpg/250px-HMS_Warrior_and_Spinnaker_Tower.jpg)](/wiki/File:HMS_Warrior_and_Spinnaker_Tower.jpg)

[HMS *Warrior*](/wiki/HMS_Warrior_(1860) "HMS Warrior (1860)") (right) and the [Spinnaker Tower](/wiki/Spinnaker_Tower "Spinnaker Tower") are two of Portsmouth's main attractions.

Many of Portsmouth's former defences are now museums or event venues. Several Victorian-era forts on Portsdown Hill are tourist attractions;[[288]](#cite_note-294) [Fort Nelson](/wiki/Fort_Nelson,_Hampshire "Fort Nelson, Hampshire"), at its summit, is home to the Royal Armouries museum.[[289]](#cite_note-295) Tudor-era Southsea Castle has a small museum, and much of the seafront defences leading to the [Round Tower](/wiki/Round_Tower_(Portsmouth) "Round Tower (Portsmouth)") are open to the public. The castle was withdrawn from active service in 1960, and was purchased by Portsmouth City Council.[[290]](#cite_note-296) The southern part of the Royal Marines' [Eastney Barracks](/wiki/Eastney_Barracks "Eastney Barracks") is now the [Royal Marines Museum](/wiki/Royal_Marines_Museum "Royal Marines Museum"), and was opened to the public under the [National Heritage Act 1983](/wiki/National_Heritage_Act_1983 "National Heritage Act 1983").[[291]](#cite_note-297) The museum received a £14 million grant from the National Lottery Fund, and was scheduled to relocate to Portsmouth Historic Dockyard in 2019.[[292]](#cite_note-298) The birthplace of [Charles Dickens](/wiki/Charles_Dickens "Charles Dickens"), at Mile End Terrace,[[293]](#cite_note-dickens-299)[[294]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHewitt201373-300) is the [Charles Dickens' Birthplace Museum](/wiki/Charles_Dickens%27_Birthplace_Museum "Charles Dickens' Birthplace Museum"); the four-storey red brick building became a Grade I listed building in 1953.[[295]](#cite_note-301) Other tourist attractions include the Blue Reef Aquarium (with an "underwater safari" of British aquatic life)[[296]](#cite_note-302) and the Cumberland House Natural History Museum, housing a variety of local wildlife.[[297]](#cite_note-303)[[298]](#cite_note-304)

[![A picture of HMS Victory, the world's oldest commissioned naval ship, situated in Portsmouth's dry dock. The ship itself is missing its figurehead in this photo, but retains its original sails.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/96/HMS_Victory.jpg/250px-HMS_Victory.jpg)](/wiki/File:HMS_Victory.jpg)

[HMS *Victory*](/wiki/HMS_Victory "HMS Victory") at Portsmouth Historic Dockyard, the world's oldest naval ship still in commission, is one of the city's most popular tourist attractions.

Most of the city's landmarks and tourist attractions are related to its naval history. They include [the D-Day Story](/wiki/The_D-Day_Story "The D-Day Story") in Southsea, which contains the 83-metre-long (272 ft) [Overlord Embroidery](/wiki/Overlord_Embroidery "Overlord Embroidery").[[299]](#cite_note-305)[[300]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHewitt2013156-306) Portsmouth is home to several well-known ships; Horatio Nelson's flagship [HMS *Victory*](/wiki/HMS_Victory "HMS Victory"), the world's oldest naval ship still in commission, is in the [dry dock](/wiki/Dry_dock "Dry dock") of [Portsmouth Historic Dockyard](/wiki/Portsmouth_Historic_Dockyard "Portsmouth Historic Dockyard"). The *Victory* was placed in a permanent dry dock in 1922 when the [Society for Nautical Research](/wiki/Society_for_Nautical_Research "Society for Nautical Research") led a national appeal to restore her,[[82]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHewitt201339-85) and 22 million people have visited the ship.[[301]](#cite_note-307) The remains of Henry VIII's flagship, *Mary Rose*, was rediscovered on the seabed in 1971.[[49]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHewitt201337-52) She was raised and brought to a purpose-built structure in Portsmouth Historic Dockyard in 1982.[[302]](#cite_note-rose-308) Britain's first iron-hulled warship, [HMS *Warrior*](/wiki/HMS_Warrior_(1860) "HMS Warrior (1860)"), was restored and moved to Portsmouth in June 1987 after serving as an oil fuel pier at [Pembroke Dock](/wiki/Pembroke_Dock "Pembroke Dock") in [Pembrokeshire](/wiki/Pembrokeshire "Pembrokeshire") for fifty years.[[303]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHewitt201342-309)[[304]](#cite_note-310)[[305]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWinton19875-311) The [National Museum of the Royal Navy](/wiki/National_Museum_of_the_Royal_Navy "National Museum of the Royal Navy"), in the dockyard, is sponsored by a charity that promotes research into the Royal Dockyard's history and archaeology.[[306]](#cite_note-312) The dockyard hosts the Victorian Festival of Christmas, featuring [Father Christmas](/wiki/Father_Christmas "Father Christmas") in a traditional green robe, each November.[[307]](#cite_note-313)[[308]](#cite_note-314)

Portsmouth's long association with the armed forces is demonstrated by a large number of war memorials, including several at the Royal Marines Museum[[309]](#cite_note-315) and a large collection of memorials related to the Royal Navy in [Victoria Park](/wiki/Victoria_Park,_Portsmouth "Victoria Park, Portsmouth").[[150]](#cite_note-vic-154) The [Portsmouth Naval Memorial](/wiki/Portsmouth_Naval_Memorial "Portsmouth Naval Memorial"), in Southsea Common, commemorates the 24,591 British sailors who died during both World Wars and have no known grave.[[310]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHewitt2013135-316) Designed by Sir [Robert Lorimer](/wiki/Robert_Lorimer "Robert Lorimer"), it was unveiled by [George VI](/wiki/George_VI "George VI") (then [Duke of York](/wiki/Duke_of_York "Duke of York")) on 15 October 1924.[[311]](#cite_note-317) In the city centre, the [Guildhall Square Cenotaph](/wiki/Guildhall_Square_Cenotaph "Guildhall Square Cenotaph") contains the names of the fallen and is guarded by stone sculptures of machine gunners by [Charles Sargeant Jagger](/wiki/Charles_Sargeant_Jagger "Charles Sargeant Jagger").[[312]](#cite_note-318) The west face of the memorial reads:

> This memorial was erected by the people of Portsmouth in proud and loving memory of those who in the glorious morning of their days for England's sake lost all but England's praise. May light perpetual shine upon them.[[313]](#cite_note-319)

The city has three cemeteries: Kingston, Milton Road, and Highland Road. Kingston Cemetery, opened in 1856, is in east Fratton. At 52 acres (21 ha), it is Portsmouth's largest cemetery and has about 400 burials a year.[[314]](#cite_note-cemeteries-320) In February 2014, a ceremony celebrating the 180th anniversary of Portsmouth's Polish community was held at the cemetery.[[315]](#cite_note-321) The approximately 25-acre (10 ha) Milton Road Cemetery, founded on 8 April 1912, has about 200 burials per year. There is a [crematorium](/wiki/Crematorium "Crematorium") in [Portchester](/wiki/Portchester "Portchester").[[314]](#cite_note-cemeteries-320)

Portsmouth Central Library in Guildhall Walk was built in 1976 in the [brutalist](/wiki/Brutalist_architecture "Brutalist architecture") style, designed by city architect Ken Norrish; it is also known as the Norrish Central Library.[[316]](#cite_note-322)[[317]](#cite_note-323)[[318]](#cite_note-324) In 2026 it was chosen by Poet Laureate [Simon Armitage](/wiki/Simon_Armitage "Simon Armitage") as one of the venues for his annual library tour which in that year visited libraries in places with initials N-P, celebrating the 50th anniversary of the library and the centenary of Portsmouth's city status.[[319]](#cite_note-325)

## Gunwharf Quays

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Portsmouth&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Gunwharf Quays")]

Main article: [Gunwharf Quays](/wiki/Gunwharf_Quays "Gunwharf Quays")

[![A view of the Spinnaker Tower from the ground at Gunwharf Quays. The tower itself resembles a sail, reflecting Portsmouth's maritime history.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/84/Spinnaker_Tower_2014.jpg/250px-Spinnaker_Tower_2014.jpg)](/wiki/File:Spinnaker_Tower_2014.jpg)

The Spinnaker Tower, seen from the waterfront at Gunwharf Quays

The naval shore establishment [HMS *Vernon*](/wiki/HMS_Vernon_(shore_establishment) "HMS Vernon (shore establishment)") contained the Royal Navy's arsenal; weapons and ammunition which would be taken from ships at its 'Gun Wharf' as they entered the harbour, and resupplied when they headed back to sea. The 1919 *Southsea and Portsmouth Official Guide* described the establishment as "the finest collections of weapons outside the Tower of London, containing more than 25,000 rifles".[[320]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHewitt2013185,_186-326) During the early nineteenth century, the 'Gunwharf' supplied the fleet with a "grand arsenal" of cannons, mortars, bombs, and ordnance. Although gunpowder was not provided due to safety concerns, it could be obtained at Priddy's Hard (near Gosport).[[321]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAllen2015123-327) An armoury sold small arms to soldiers, and the [stone frigate](/wiki/Stone_frigate "Stone frigate") also had blacksmith and carpenter shops for armourers. It was run by three officers: a *viz* (storekeeper), a clerk, and a foreman. By 1817, Gunwharf reportedly employed 5,000 men and housed the world's largest naval arsenal.[[322]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAllen2015123,_124-328)

HMS *Vernon* was closed on 1 April 1996[[323]](#cite_note-vernon-329) and was redeveloped by Portsmouth City Council as Gunwharf Quays,[[196]](#cite_note-guns-202) a mixed residential and retail site with [outlet stores](/wiki/Outlet_store "Outlet store"), restaurants, pubs, cafés and a cinema.[[324]](#cite_note-330) Construction of the [Spinnaker Tower](/wiki/Spinnaker_Tower "Spinnaker Tower") began in 2001, and was completed in the summer of 2005. The project exceeded its budget and cost £36 million, of which Portsmouth City Council contributed £11 million.[[325]](#cite_note-331)[[326]](#cite_note-332)[[327]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHewitt2013232-333) The 560-foot (170 m) tower is visible at a distance of 23 miles (37 km) in clear weather, and its viewing platforms overlook the Solent (towards the Isle of Wight), the harbour and Southsea Castle.[[328]](#cite_note-334)[[329]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHewitt2013233-335) The tower weighs over 33,000 tonnes (32,000 long tons; 36,000 short tons).[[330]](#cite_note-336)[[329]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHewitt2013233-335)and has the largest glass floor in Europe.

## Southsea

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Portsmouth&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Southsea")]

Main article: [Southsea](/wiki/Southsea "Southsea")

[![A view of the Southsea Promenade, which contains arcades, restaurants, cinemas and a pier (which cannot be seen in this photograph)](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/60/Southsea_Clarence_Pier.jpg/250px-Southsea_Clarence_Pier.jpg)](/wiki/File:Southsea_Clarence_Pier.jpg)

Southsea Promenade, which includes the Clarence Pier amusement park

[![The Portsmouth Naval Memorial in Southsea: a large stone pillar and a plaque commemorating the fallen sailors of both World Wars](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4e/Southsea_War_Memorial_-_geograph.org.uk_-_1297412.jpg/250px-Southsea_War_Memorial_-_geograph.org.uk_-_1297412.jpg)](/wiki/File:Southsea_War_Memorial_-_geograph.org.uk_-_1297412.jpg)

[Portsmouth Naval Memorial](/wiki/Portsmouth_Naval_Memorial "Portsmouth Naval Memorial") in Southsea

Southsea is a [seaside resort](/wiki/Seaside_resort "Seaside resort") and residential area of Portsmouth located at the southern end of Portsea Island. Its name originates from Southsea Castle, a seafront castle built in 1544 by Henry VIII to help defend the [Solent](/wiki/Solent "Solent") and Portsmouth Harbour.[[331]](#cite_note-337) The area was developed in 1809 as Croxton Town; by the 1860s, the suburb of Southsea had expanded to provide working-class housing.[[135]](#cite_note-pissea-138) Southsea developed as a seaside and bathing resort.[[135]](#cite_note-pissea-138) A pump room and baths were built near the present-day [Clarence Pier](/wiki/Clarence_Pier "Clarence Pier"), and a complex was developed which included vapour baths, showers, and card-playing and assembly rooms for holiday-goers.[[332]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEQuail200016–17-338)

Clarence Pier, opened in 1861 by the [Prince](/wiki/Edward_VII "Edward VII") and [Princess of Wales](/wiki/Alexandra_of_Denmark "Alexandra of Denmark"), was named after Portsmouth military governor [Lord Frederick FitzClarence](/wiki/Lord_Frederick_FitzClarence "Lord Frederick FitzClarence") and was described as "one of the largest amusement parks on the south coast".[[333]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHewitt2013140-339) [South Parade Pier](/wiki/South_Parade_Pier "South Parade Pier") was built in 1878, and is among the United Kingdom's 55 remaining private piers.[[334]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHewitt2013138-340)[[335]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEQuail200046-341) Originally a terminal for ferries travelling to the Isle of Wight, it was soon redeveloped as an entertainment centre. The pier was rebuilt after fires in 1904, 1967 and 1974 (during the filming of *[Tommy](/wiki/Tommy_(1975_film) "Tommy (1975 film)")*).[[334]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHewitt2013138-340)[[135]](#cite_note-pissea-138) Plans were announced in 2015 for a Solent Eye at Clarence Pier: a £750,000, 24-gondola [Ferris wheel](/wiki/Ferris_wheel "Ferris wheel") similar to the [London Eye](/wiki/London_Eye "London Eye").[[336]](#cite_note-342)

Southsea is dominated by Southsea Common, a 480-acre (190 ha) grassland created by draining the marshland next to the vapour baths in 1820. The common met the demands of the early-19th-century military for a clear [firing range](/wiki/Shooting_range "Shooting range"),[[337]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEQuail200019–20-343) and parallels the shore from Clarence Pier to Southsea Castle.[[337]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEQuail200019–20-343) A popular recreation area, it hosts a number of annual events which include carnivals, Christmas markets, and Victorian festivals.[[338]](#cite_note-344)[[339]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHewitt2013136-345) The common has a large collection of mature [elm](/wiki/Elm "Elm") trees, believed to be the oldest and largest surviving in Hampshire and which have escaped [Dutch elm disease](/wiki/Dutch_elm_disease "Dutch elm disease") due to their isolation. Other plants include the [Canary Island](/wiki/Canary_Island "Canary Island") date palms (*[Phoenix canariensis](/wiki/Phoenix_canariensis "Phoenix canariensis")*), some of Britain's largest, which have recently produced viable seed.[[340]](#cite_note-346)

The resort of Southsea previously had its own dedicated light railway line; the [Southsea Railway](/wiki/Southsea_Railway "Southsea Railway") and its own terminus, [East Southsea railway station](/wiki/East_Southsea_railway_station "East Southsea railway station"). The Southsea Railway and station were closed in 1914, with the station's name merged into that of Portsmouth's main railway station name in 1925.

## Religion

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Portsmouth&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Religion")]

See also: [List of places of worship in Portsmouth](/wiki/List_of_places_of_worship_in_Portsmouth "List of places of worship in Portsmouth")

[![A front facing view of Portsmouth's Roman Catholic cathedral, St John the Evangelist. The cathedral itself is made of brick and has a large chancel and nave at the front. Stained windows are also seen above the front door.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c5/Portsmouthcatholiccathedral.jpg/250px-Portsmouthcatholiccathedral.jpg)](/wiki/File:Portsmouthcatholiccathedral.jpg)

St John the Evangelist, the Roman Catholic cathedral built in 1882, is one of the city's two cathedrals.

Portsmouth has two cathedrals: the [Anglican Cathedral of St Thomas](/wiki/Portsmouth_Cathedral "Portsmouth Cathedral") in [Old Portsmouth](/wiki/Old_Portsmouth "Old Portsmouth") and the Roman Catholic [Cathedral of St John the Evangelist](/wiki/Cathedral_of_St_John_the_Evangelist,_Portsmouth "Cathedral of St John the Evangelist, Portsmouth"). The city is one of 34 British settlements with a [Roman Catholic](/wiki/Roman_Catholic "Roman Catholic") cathedral.[[165]](#cite_note-cathhh-170)[[341]](#cite_note-347) Portsmouth's first chapel, dedicated to [Thomas Becket](/wiki/Thomas_Becket "Thomas Becket"), was built by Jean de Gisors in the second half of the 12th century.[[342]](#cite_note-348)[[343]](#cite_note-chapel2-349) It was rebuilt and developed into a [parish church](/wiki/Parish_church "Parish church") and an Anglican cathedral.[[343]](#cite_note-chapel2-349)[[344]](#cite_note-350) Damaged during the 1642 [Siege of Portsmouth](/wiki/Siege_of_Portsmouth "Siege of Portsmouth"), its tower and [nave](/wiki/Nave "Nave") were rebuilt after [the Restoration](/wiki/Stuart_Restoration "Stuart Restoration").[[345]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKnowles200621-351) Significant changes were made when the Diocese of Portsmouth was founded in 1927.[[346]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHewitt201344-352) It became a cathedral in 1932 and was enlarged, although construction was halted during the Second World War. The cathedral was re-consecrated before [Queen Elizabeth The Queen Mother](/wiki/Queen_Elizabeth_The_Queen_Mother "Queen Elizabeth The Queen Mother") in 1991.[[347]](#cite_note-353)

The [Royal Garrison Church](/wiki/Domus_Dei "Domus Dei") was founded in 1212 by [Peter des Roches](/wiki/Peter_des_Roches "Peter des Roches"), [Bishop of Winchester](/wiki/Bishop_of_Winchester "Bishop of Winchester"). After centuries of decay, it became an ammunition store in 1540. The 1662 marriage of Charles II and [Catherine of Braganza](/wiki/Catherine_of_Braganza "Catherine of Braganza") was celebrated in the church, and large receptions were held there after the defeat of [Napoleon](/wiki/Napoleon "Napoleon") at the 1813 [Battle of Leipzig](/wiki/Battle_of_Leipzig "Battle of Leipzig"). In 1941, a firebomb fell on its roof and destroyed the nave.[[57]](#cite_note-garr-60) Although the church's chancel was saved by servicemen shortly after the raid, replacing the roof was deemed impossible due to the large amounts of salt solution absorbed by the stonework.[[348]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHewitt2013150-354)

The Cathedral of St John the Evangelist was built in 1882 to accommodate Portsmouth's increasing Roman Catholic population, and replaced a chapel built in 1796 to the west. Before 1791, Roman Catholic chapels in towns with [borough status](/wiki/Borough_status_in_the_United_Kingdom "Borough status in the United Kingdom") were prohibited. The chapel opened after the [Roman Catholic Relief Act 1791](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Relief_Act_1791 "Roman Catholic Relief Act 1791") was passed, and was replaced by the cathedral.[[349]](#cite_note-catholic-355) It was constructed in phases; the nave was completed in 1882; the [crossing](/wiki/Crossing_(architecture) "Crossing (architecture)") in 1886, and the [chancel](/wiki/Chancel "Chancel") by 1893. During the blitz, the cathedral was badly damaged when Luftwaffe bombing destroyed Bishop's House next door; it was restored in 1970, 1982, and 2001.[[349]](#cite_note-catholic-355) The [Roman Catholic Diocese of Portsmouth](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Diocese_of_Portsmouth "Roman Catholic Diocese of Portsmouth") was founded in 1882 by [Pope Leo XIII](/wiki/Pope_Leo_XIII "Pope Leo XIII").[[g]](#cite_note-357) Smaller places of worship in the city include St Jude's Church in Southsea,[[351]](#cite_note-358) [St Mary's Church](/wiki/St_Mary%27s_Church,_Portsea "St Mary's Church, Portsea") in Portsea,[[352]](#cite_note-359) [St Ann's Chapel](/wiki/St_Ann%27s_Church,_HMNB_Portsmouth "St Ann's Church, HMNB Portsmouth") in the naval base[[353]](#cite_note-360) and the [Portsmouth and Southsea Synagogue](/wiki/Portsmouth_and_Southsea_Synagogue "Portsmouth and Southsea Synagogue"), one of Britain's oldest.[[354]](#cite_note-361) Other places of worship include the Immanuel Baptist Church, Southsea; Trinity Methodist Church, Highland Road; Buckland United Reformed Church; The Oasis Centre Elim Penteostal Church; Jubilee Pentecostal Church, Somers Road; Kings Church Assemblies of God (St Peter's Somers Road); Family Church; Christ Central Church, John Pounds Centre; The Jami Mosque, Bradford Junction; The Sikh Gurudwara, Margate Road.

## Sport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Portsmouth&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Sport")]

[![Fratton Park football stadium at night, home of Portsmouth F.C. The pitch is lit by floodlights.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3a/Fratton_Park%2C_Sep_2006.jpg/250px-Fratton_Park%2C_Sep_2006.jpg)](/wiki/File:Fratton_Park,_Sep_2006.jpg)

Fratton Park, home of Portsmouth F.C.

[Portsmouth F.C.](/wiki/Portsmouth_F.C. "Portsmouth F.C.") play their home games at [Fratton Park](/wiki/Fratton_Park "Fratton Park"). They have won two [Football League](/wiki/Football_League "Football League") titles (1949 and 1950),[[355]](#cite_note-362)[[356]](#cite_note-rsssf-363) and won the [FA Cup](/wiki/FA_Cup "FA Cup") in 1939 and 2008.[[357]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENeasom198421-364)[[358]](#cite_note-365) The club returned to the [Premier League](/wiki/Premier_League "Premier League") in 2003.[[359]](#cite_note-366) They were relegated to the [Championship](/wiki/Football_League_Championship "Football League Championship") in 2010 and, experiencing serious financial difficulties in February 2012,[[360]](#cite_note-367) were relegated again to [League One](/wiki/Football_League_One "Football League One"). The club was relegated the following year to [League Two](/wiki/EFL_League_Two "EFL League Two"), the fourth tier of [English football](/wiki/Football_in_England#Men's_League_system "Football in England").[[361]](#cite_note-368) Portsmouth F.C. was purchased in April 2013 by the Pompey Supporters Trust, becoming the largest fan-owned club in English Football history.[[362]](#cite_note-369)[[363]](#cite_note-370) In May 2017, as League Two champions, they were promoted to [League One](/wiki/EFL_League_One "EFL League One") for the 2017–18 season. They won promotion back to the Championship as Champions of League One in May 2024.

[Moneyfields F.C.](/wiki/Moneyfields_F.C. "Moneyfields F.C.") have played in the [Wessex Football League](/wiki/Wessex_Football_League "Wessex Football League") Premier Division since 1998.[[364]](#cite_note-371) [United Services Portsmouth F.C.](/wiki/United_Services_Portsmouth_F.C. "United Services Portsmouth F.C.") (formerly known as Portsmouth Royal Navy) and [Baffins Milton Rovers F.C.](/wiki/Baffins_Milton_Rovers_F.C. "Baffins Milton Rovers F.C.") compete in Wessex League Division One; United Services was founded in 1962,[[365]](#cite_note-372) and Baffins Milton Rovers in 2011.[[366]](#cite_note-373) The [rugby](/wiki/Rugby_union "Rugby union") teams [United Services Portsmouth RFC](/wiki/United_Services_Portsmouth_Rugby_Football_Club "United Services Portsmouth Rugby Football Club") and [Royal Navy Rugby Union](/wiki/Royal_Navy_Rugby_Union "Royal Navy Rugby Union") play their home matches at the [United Services Recreation Ground](/wiki/United_Services_Recreation_Ground "United Services Recreation Ground"). Royal Navy Rugby Union play in the annual [Army Navy Match](/wiki/Army_Navy_Match "Army Navy Match") at [Twickenham](/wiki/Twickenham "Twickenham").[[367]](#cite_note-374)

Portsmouth began hosting [first-class cricket](/wiki/First-class_cricket "First-class cricket") at the United Services Recreation Ground in 1882,[[368]](#cite_note-375) and [Hampshire County Cricket Club](/wiki/Hampshire_County_Cricket_Club "Hampshire County Cricket Club") matches were played there from 1895 to 2000. In 2000, Hampshire moved their home matches to the new [Rose Bowl](/wiki/Rose_Bowl_(cricket_ground) "Rose Bowl (cricket ground)") cricket ground in [West End](/wiki/West_End,_Hampshire "West End, Hampshire").[[369]](#cite_note-PRO-376) Portsmouth is home to two [hockey](/wiki/Field_hockey "Field hockey") clubs: Portsmouth Hockey Club, based at the [Admiral Lord Nelson School](/wiki/Admiral_Lord_Nelson_School "Admiral Lord Nelson School") and United Services Portsmouth Hockey Club, based on Burnaby Road.[[370]](#cite_note-377) Great Salterns Golf Club, established in 1926,[[371]](#cite_note-378) is an 18-hole parkland course with two holes played across a lake;[[372]](#cite_note-379) there are coastal courses at [Hayling](/w/index.php?title=Hayling_Golf_Club&action=edit&redlink=1 "Hayling Golf Club (page does not exist)") and the Gosport and Stokes Bay Golf Club.[[124]](#cite_note-GM-127) Boxing was a popular sport between 1910 and 1960, and a monument commemorating the city's boxing heritage was built in 2017.[[373]](#cite_note-380)

## Transport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Portsmouth&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Transport")]

Main article: [Transport in South Hampshire](/wiki/Transport_in_South_Hampshire "Transport in South Hampshire")

### Roads

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Portsmouth&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Roads")]

In March 2008, Portsmouth City Council became the first local authority in the UK to implement city-wide 20 miles per hour speed limit zones.[[374]](#cite_note-381)[[375]](#cite_note-382)

### Ferries

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Portsmouth&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Ferries")]

[![A view of various ferries, cargo and military vessels moving out of Portsmouth Harbour. This photograph was taken from the viewing deck of the Spinnaker Tower.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/43/A_busy_scene_with_ferries_-_geograph.org.uk_-_1306443.jpg/250px-A_busy_scene_with_ferries_-_geograph.org.uk_-_1306443.jpg)](/wiki/File:A_busy_scene_with_ferries_-_geograph.org.uk_-_1306443.jpg)

Ferries and cargo and military vessels in Portsmouth Harbour

[Portsmouth Harbour](/wiki/Portsmouth_Harbour_railway_station "Portsmouth Harbour railway station") has passenger-ferry links to [Gosport](/wiki/Gosport "Gosport") and the [Isle of Wight](/wiki/Isle_of_Wight "Isle of Wight"),[[376]](#cite_note-383) with car-ferry service to the Isle of Wight nearby.[[377]](#cite_note-384) [Hovertravel](/wiki/Hovertravel "Hovertravel"), Britain's longest-standing commercial [hovercraft](/wiki/Hovercraft "Hovercraft") service, begun in the 1960s, runs from near Clarence Pier in [Southsea](/wiki/Southsea "Southsea") to [Ryde](/wiki/Ryde "Ryde"), Isle of Wight.[[378]](#cite_note-385) [Portsmouth International Port](/wiki/Portsmouth_International_Port "Portsmouth International Port") has links to [Caen](/wiki/Caen "Caen"), [Cherbourg-Octeville](/wiki/Cherbourg-Octeville "Cherbourg-Octeville"), [St Malo](/wiki/St_Malo "St Malo") and [Le Havre](/wiki/Le_Havre "Le Havre") in France,[[379]](#cite_note-386)[[380]](#cite_note-387) [Santander](/wiki/Santander,_Cantabria "Santander, Cantabria") and [Bilbao](/wiki/Bilbao "Bilbao") in Spain,[[381]](#cite_note-bit-388) and the [Channel Islands](/wiki/Channel_Islands "Channel Islands").[[382]](#cite_note-chan-389) Ferry services from the port are operated by [Brittany Ferries](/wiki/Brittany_Ferries "Brittany Ferries") and [DFDS Seaways](/wiki/DFDS_Seaways "DFDS Seaways").[[383]](#cite_note-390)

On 18 May 2006, [Trasmediterranea](/wiki/Trasmediterranea "Trasmediterranea") began service to Bilbao in competition with [P&O](/wiki/P%26O_Ferries "P&O Ferries")'s service. Its ferry, *Fortuny*, was detained in Portsmouth by the [Maritime and Coastguard Agency](/wiki/Maritime_and_Coastguard_Agency "Maritime and Coastguard Agency") for a number of safety violations.[[384]](#cite_note-391) They were quickly corrected and the service was cleared for passengers on 23 May that year.[[385]](#cite_note-392) Trasmediterránea discontinued its Bilbao service in March 2007, citing a need to deploy the *Fortuny* elsewhere.[[386]](#cite_note-393) P&O Ferries ended their service to [Bilbao](/wiki/Bilbao "Bilbao") on 27 September 2010 due to "unsustainable losses".[[387]](#cite_note-394)[[388]](#cite_note-395) The second-busiest ferry port in the UK (after [Dover](/wiki/Dover "Dover")), Portsmouth handles about three million passengers per year.[[389]](#cite_note-396)[[390]](#cite_note-397)

### Buses

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Portsmouth&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Buses")]

See also: [Buses in Portsmouth](/wiki/Buses_in_Portsmouth "Buses in Portsmouth")

Local bus services are provided by [Stagecoach South](/wiki/Stagecoach_South "Stagecoach South") and [First Hampshire & Dorset](/wiki/First_Hampshire_%26_Dorset "First Hampshire & Dorset") to the city and its surrounding towns. Hovertravel and First Hampshire & Dorset operate a Hoverbus service from the city centre to Southsea Hovercraft Terminal and the Hard Interchange, near the seafront.[[391]](#cite_note-398) [National Express](/wiki/National_Express_Coaches "National Express Coaches") service from Portsmouth operates primarily from the Hard Interchange to [Victoria Coach Station](/wiki/Victoria_Coach_Station "Victoria Coach Station"), Cornwall, [Bradford](/wiki/Bradford_Interchange "Bradford Interchange"), [Birkenhead](/wiki/Birkenhead "Birkenhead") and [Bristol](/wiki/Bristol_bus_station "Bristol bus station").[[392]](#cite_note-399)

Portsmouth has four railway stations on Portsea Island: [Hilsea](/wiki/Hilsea_railway_station "Hilsea railway station"), [Fratton](/wiki/Fratton_railway_station "Fratton railway station"), [Portsmouth & Southsea](/wiki/Portsmouth_%26_Southsea_railway_station "Portsmouth & Southsea railway station")[[393]](#cite_note-400) and [Portsmouth Harbour](/wiki/Portsmouth_Harbour_railway_station "Portsmouth Harbour railway station"),[[394]](#cite_note-401) with a fifth station at [Cosham](/wiki/Cosham_railway_station "Cosham railway station") in the northern mainland suburb of [Cosham, Portsmouth](/wiki/Cosham "Cosham"). Portsmouth previously had additional stations at [Southsea](/wiki/East_Southsea_railway_station "East Southsea railway station"), [Farlington](/wiki/Farlington_Halt_railway_station "Farlington Halt railway station") and [Paulsgrove](/wiki/Paulsgrove_Halt_railway_station "Paulsgrove Halt railway station"), but these were closed at various periods of the twentieth century.

The city of Portsmouth is on two direct [South Western Railway](/wiki/South_Western_Railway_(train_operating_company) "South Western Railway (train operating company)") routes to [London Waterloo](/wiki/London_Waterloo_railway_station "London Waterloo railway station"), via [Guildford](/wiki/Guildford_railway_station "Guildford railway station") and via [Basingstoke](/wiki/Basingstoke_railway_station "Basingstoke railway station").[[395]](#cite_note-402) There is a South Western Railway stopping service to [Southampton Central](/wiki/Southampton_Central_railway_station "Southampton Central railway station") and [Great Western Railway](/wiki/Great_Western_Railway_(train_operating_company) "Great Western Railway (train operating company)") service to [Cardiff Central](/wiki/Cardiff_Central_railway_station "Cardiff Central railway station") via Southampton, [Salisbury](/wiki/Salisbury_railway_station "Salisbury railway station"), [Bath Spa](/wiki/Bath_Spa_railway_station "Bath Spa railway station") and [Bristol](/wiki/Bristol_Temple_Meads_railway_station "Bristol Temple Meads railway station").[[396]](#cite_note-403) [Southern](/wiki/Southern_(train_operating_company) "Southern (train operating company)") has service to [Brighton](/wiki/Brighton_railway_station "Brighton railway station"), [Gatwick Airport](/wiki/Gatwick_Airport_railway_station "Gatwick Airport railway station"), [Croydon](/wiki/East_Croydon_railway_station "East Croydon railway station") and [London Victoria](/wiki/London_Victoria_railway_station "London Victoria railway station").[[397]](#cite_note-404)

#### Closed stations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Portsmouth&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Closed stations")]

Southsea once had its own branch line, the [Southsea Railway](/wiki/Southsea_Railway "Southsea Railway"), which opened in 1885 between [Southsea railway station](/wiki/East_Southsea_railway_station "East Southsea railway station") and Fratton; it was closed in 1914 due to competition from tram services.[[398]](#cite_note-405)

[Farlington Halt railway station](/wiki/Farlington_Halt_railway_station "Farlington Halt railway station") was built to serve Portsmouth Park [racecourse](/wiki/Horseracing_in_the_United_Kingdom "Horseracing in the United Kingdom"), opening as Farlington Race Course on 26 June 1891.[[399]](#cite_note-Butt-406) The racecourse was closed during [World War One](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I"), but the station was retained to serve the ammunition dump put in its place.[[400]](#cite_note-407) The station closed in 1917.[[399]](#cite_note-Butt-406) Re-opened in 1922 until 1927.[[399]](#cite_note-Butt-406) Under the [Southern Railway](/wiki/Southern_Railway_(UK) "Southern Railway (UK)"), it re-opened as a general public [halt](/wiki/Railway_halt "Railway halt") in 1928 named *Farlington Halt*;[[399]](#cite_note-Butt-406) however, this was short-lived as the station closed due to insufficient customers on 4 July 1937.[[399]](#cite_note-Butt-406)

[Paulsgrove Halt railway station](/wiki/Paulsgrove_Halt_railway_station "Paulsgrove Halt railway station")[[401]](#cite_note-408) was a railway station opened in 1928 to serve the adjacent Portsmouth [Racecourse](/wiki/Racecourse "Racecourse"), a pony racing stronghold.[[402]](#cite_note-409) The station was formerly located between Cosham and Portchester stations. Paulsgrove Halt was closed along with the racecourse when the land was acquired by the military in 1939, at the outbreak of [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II").

### Air

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Portsmouth&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Air")]

[Portsmouth Airport](/wiki/Portsmouth_Airport_(Hampshire) "Portsmouth Airport (Hampshire)"), with a grass runway, was in operation from 1932 to 1973. After it closed, housing (Anchorage Park) and industry were built on the site.[[403]](#cite_note-410)[[404]](#cite_note-411) The nearest airport is [Southampton Airport](/wiki/Southampton_Airport "Southampton Airport") in the [Borough of Eastleigh](/wiki/Borough_of_Eastleigh "Borough of Eastleigh"), 19.8 miles (31.9 km) away.[[124]](#cite_note-GM-127) It has a [South Western Railway](/wiki/South_Western_Railway_(train_operating_company) "South Western Railway (train operating company)") rail connection, requiring a change at [Southampton Central](/wiki/Southampton_Central_railway_station "Southampton Central railway station") or [Eastleigh](/wiki/Eastleigh_railway_station "Eastleigh railway station").[[405]](#cite_note-412) [Heathrow](/wiki/Heathrow_Airport "Heathrow Airport") and [Gatwick](/wiki/Gatwick_Airport "Gatwick Airport") are 65 miles (105 km) and 75 miles (121 km) away, respectively. Gatwick is linked by Southern train service to London Victoria station and Heathrow is linked by coach to [Woking](/wiki/Woking "Woking"), which is on both rail lines to London Waterloo and the [London Underground](/wiki/London_Underground "London Underground").[[406]](#cite_note-413) Heathrow is linked to Portsmouth by National Express coaches.[[407]](#cite_note-414)

### Former canal

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Portsmouth&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Former canal")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/34/Portsea_islandcanal1815.jpg/250px-Portsea_islandcanal1815.jpg)](/wiki/File:Portsea_islandcanal1815.jpg)

A map of the planned route of Portsmouth and Arundel Canal across Portsea Island from 1815

The [Portsmouth and Arundel Canal](/wiki/Portsmouth_and_Arundel_Canal "Portsmouth and Arundel Canal") ran between the towns and was built in 1823 by the Portsmouth & Arundel Navigation Company. Never financially successful, and found to be contaminating Portsea Island fresh water wells,[[408]](#cite_note-415) it was abandoned in 1855 and the company was wound up in 1888.[[409]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGreen200618–19-416) The canal was part of a larger scheme for a secure inland canal route from London to Portsmouth, allowing boats to avoid the [English Channel](/wiki/English_Channel "English Channel"). It had three sections: a pair of ship canals (one on Portsea Island and one to [Chichester](/wiki/Chichester "Chichester")) and a barge canal from [Ford](/wiki/Ford,_West_Sussex "Ford, West Sussex") on the [River Arun](/wiki/River_Arun "River Arun") to [Hunston](/wiki/Hunston,_West_Sussex "Hunston, West Sussex"), where it joined the canal's Chichester section.[[410]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEArmstrong1971144–145-417)

The route through Portsea Island began from a basin formerly located on Arundel Street and cut through [Landport](/wiki/Landport "Landport"), Fratton and [Milton](/wiki/Milton,_Portsmouth "Milton, Portsmouth"), ending at the eastern end of Locksway Road in Milton (where a set of lock gates accessed [Langstone](/wiki/Langstone_Harbour "Langstone Harbour") and [Chichester Harbours](/wiki/Chichester_Harbour "Chichester Harbour"). After the island route was closed, the drained canal-bed sections through Landport and Fratton were reused for the [Portsmouth Direct line](/wiki/Portsmouth_Direct_line "Portsmouth Direct line"), or filled-in to surface level to form a new main road route to Milton, named Goldsmith Avenue.

The brick-lined canal walls are clearly visible between the Fratton and Portsmouth & Southsea railway stations. The canal lock entrance at Locksway Road in Milton is east of the Thatched House [pub](/wiki/Pub "Pub").[[411]](#cite_note-418)

## Media

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Portsmouth&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Media")]

Portsmouth, Southampton and their adjacent towns are served primarily by programming from the [Rowridge](/wiki/Rowridge_transmitting_station "Rowridge transmitting station") and [Chillerton Down](/wiki/Chillerton_Down_transmitting_station "Chillerton Down transmitting station") transmitters on the [Isle of Wight](/wiki/Isle_of_Wight "Isle of Wight"),[[412]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPawley1972-419)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*] although the transmitter at [Midhurst](/wiki/Midhurst_transmitting_station "Midhurst transmitting station") can substitute for Rowridge. Portsmouth was one of the first cities in the UK to have a local TV station (MyTV), although the Isle of Wight began local television broadcasting in 1998.[[413]](#cite_note-420) In November 2014, [That's Solent](/wiki/That%27s_Solent "That's Solent") was introduced as part of a nationwide roll-out of local [Freeview](/wiki/Freeview_(UK) "Freeview (UK)") channels in south-central England.[[414]](#cite_note-421) The stations broadcast from Rowridge.[[415]](#cite_note-422)

[BBC](/wiki/BBC "BBC") local radio station that broadcast to the city is [BBC Radio Solent](/wiki/BBC_Radio_Solent "BBC Radio Solent") on 96.1 FM. According to [RAJAR](/wiki/RAJAR "RAJAR"), popular radio stations include regional [Greatest Hits Radio South](/wiki/Greatest_Hits_Radio_South "Greatest Hits Radio South") and Global Radio's [Heart South](/wiki/Heart_South "Heart South") and [Capital South](/wiki/Capital_South "Capital South"). [Easy Radio South Coast](/wiki/Easy_Radio_South_Coast "Easy Radio South Coast") broadcasts from Southampton to the city on 107.4 MHz,[[416]](#cite_note-423) and the non-profit community station, Express FM, broadcasts on 93.7.[[417]](#cite_note-424) Patients at [Queen Alexandra Hospital](/wiki/Queen_Alexandra_Hospital "Queen Alexandra Hospital") (Portsmouth's primary hospital) receive local programming from Portsmouth Hospital Broadcasting, which began in 1951.[[418]](#cite_note-425) When the first local commercial radio stations were licensed during the 1970s by the [Independent Broadcasting Authority](/wiki/Independent_Broadcasting_Authority "Independent Broadcasting Authority") (IBA), [Radio Victory](/wiki/Radio_Victory "Radio Victory") received the first licence and began broadcasting in 1975. In 1986, the IBA increased the Portsmouth licence to include Southampton and the Isle of Wight. The new licence went to Ocean Sound (later known as Ocean FM), with studios in [Fareham](/wiki/Fareham "Fareham"); Ocean FM became [Heart Hampshire](/wiki/Heart_Hampshire "Heart Hampshire"). For the city's 800th birthday in 1994, Victory FM broadcast for three 28-day periods over 18 months.[[419]](#cite_note-426) It was purchased by TLRC, who relaunched the station in 2001 as the Quay;[[420]](#cite_note-427) Portsmouth Football Club became a stakeholder in 2007, selling it in 2009.[[421]](#cite_note-428)

Portsmouth's daily newspaper is *[The News](/wiki/The_News_(Portsmouth) "The News (Portsmouth)")*, founded in 1873 and previously known as the *Portsmouth Evening News*. *The Journal*, a free weekly newspaper, is published by *News* publisher [Johnston Press](/wiki/Johnston_Press "Johnston Press").[[422]](#cite_note-429)[[423]](#cite_note-430)

## Notable residents

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Portsmouth&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Notable residents")]

See also: [List of people from Portsmouth](/wiki/List_of_people_from_Portsmouth "List of people from Portsmouth")

Portsmouth has been home to a number of famed authors; [Charles Dickens](/wiki/Charles_Dickens "Charles Dickens"), whose works include *[A Christmas Carol](/wiki/A_Christmas_Carol "A Christmas Carol")*, *[Great Expectations](/wiki/Great_Expectations "Great Expectations")*, *[Oliver Twist](/wiki/Oliver_Twist "Oliver Twist")* and *[A Tale of Two Cities](/wiki/A_Tale_of_Two_Cities "A Tale of Two Cities")*, was born there.[[424]](#cite_note-431) [Arthur Conan Doyle](/wiki/Arthur_Conan_Doyle "Arthur Conan Doyle"), author of the [Sherlock Holmes](/wiki/Sherlock_Holmes "Sherlock Holmes") stories, practised medicine in the city and played in goal for the amateur Portsmouth Association Football Club.[[425]](#cite_note-432) [Rudyard Kipling](/wiki/Rudyard_Kipling "Rudyard Kipling") (poet and author of *[The Jungle Book](/wiki/The_Jungle_Book "The Jungle Book")*)[[426]](#cite_note-blue-433) and [H. G. Wells](/wiki/H._G._Wells "H. G. Wells"), author of *[The War of the Worlds](/wiki/The_War_of_the_Worlds "The War of the Worlds")* and *[The Time Machine](/wiki/The_Time_Machine "The Time Machine")*, lived in Portsmouth during the 1880s.[[427]](#cite_note-434) Novelist and historian [Walter Besant](/wiki/Walter_Besant "Walter Besant"), author of *By Celia's Arbour, A Tale of Portsmouth Town*, was born in Portsmouth.[[428]](#cite_note-435)[[429]](#cite_note-436) Historian [Frances Yates](/wiki/Frances_Yates "Frances Yates"), known for her work on Renaissance [esotericism](/wiki/Western_esotericism "Western esotericism"), was born in the city.[[430]](#cite_note-437)[Francis Austen](/wiki/Francis_Austen "Francis Austen"), brother of [Jane Austen](/wiki/Jane_Austen "Jane Austen"), briefly lived in the area after graduating from Portsmouth Naval Academy.[[431]](#cite_note-438) Contemporary literary figures include social critic, journalist and author [Christopher Hitchens](/wiki/Christopher_Hitchens "Christopher Hitchens"), who was born in Portsmouth.[[432]](#cite_note-439) [Nevil Shute](/wiki/Nevil_Shute "Nevil Shute") moved to the city in 1934 when he relocated his aircraft company, and his former home is in Southsea.[[433]](#cite_note-440) Fantasy author [Neil Gaiman](/wiki/Neil_Gaiman "Neil Gaiman") grew up in [Purbrook](/wiki/Purbrook "Purbrook") and Southsea.[[263]](#cite_note-Flood-269)[[434]](#cite_note-Bowlby-441)

[Industrial Revolution](/wiki/Industrial_Revolution "Industrial Revolution") engineer [Isambard Kingdom Brunel](/wiki/Isambard_Kingdom_Brunel "Isambard Kingdom Brunel") was born in Portsmouth.[[435]](#cite_note-442)[[436]](#cite_note-443) His father, [Marc Isambard Brunel](/wiki/Marc_Isambard_Brunel "Marc Isambard Brunel"), worked for the Royal Navy and developed the world's first [production line](/wiki/Production_line "Production line") to mass-produce [pulley blocks](/wiki/Block_(sailing) "Block (sailing)") for ship rigging.[[75]](#cite_note-royal-78) [James Callaghan](/wiki/James_Callaghan "James Callaghan"), British prime minister from 1976 to 1979, was born and raised in Portsmouth.[[437]](#cite_note-444)[[438]](#cite_note-445) Son of a Protestant Northern Irish petty officer in the Royal Navy, Callaghan was the only person to hold all four [Great Offices of State](/wiki/Great_Offices_of_State "Great Offices of State"): foreign secretary, home secretary, chancellor and prime minister.[[439]](#cite_note-446) [John Pounds](/wiki/John_Pounds "John Pounds"), the founder of [ragged schools](/wiki/Ragged_school "Ragged school") (which provided free education to working-class children), lived in Portsmouth and founded England's first ragged school there.[[440]](#cite_note-447)

Comedian and actor [Peter Sellers](/wiki/Peter_Sellers "Peter Sellers") was born in Southsea,[[441]](#cite_note-448) and [Arnold Schwarzenegger](/wiki/Arnold_Schwarzenegger "Arnold Schwarzenegger") briefly lived and trained in Portsmouth.[[442]](#cite_note-449) Other actors who were born or lived in the city include *[EastEnders](/wiki/EastEnders "EastEnders")* actresses [Emma Barton](/wiki/Emma_Barton "Emma Barton")[[443]](#cite_note-450) and [Lorraine Stanley](/wiki/Lorraine_Stanley "Lorraine Stanley"), comedienne[[444]](#cite_note-451) and singer [Audrey Jeans](/wiki/Audrey_Jeans "Audrey Jeans"),[[445]](#cite_note-452) and Bollywood actress [Geeta Basra](/wiki/Geeta_Basra "Geeta Basra").[[446]](#cite_note-453) Cryptozoologist Jonathan Downes was born in Portsmouth, and lived there for a time.[[447]](#cite_note-454) [Ant Middleton](/wiki/Ant_Middleton "Ant Middleton"), former SBS, current television presenter and author was born in Portsmouth.[[448]](#cite_note-455)
[Helen Duncan](/wiki/Helen_Duncan "Helen Duncan"), the last person to be imprisoned under the 1735 Witchcraft Act, was arrested in Portsmouth.[[449]](#cite_note-456) Notable [content creation](/wiki/Content_creation "Content creation") and [youth activism](/wiki/Youth_activism "Youth activism") duo Henry Russell and Austin Taylor were born and raised in Portsmouth and work to improve the city for young people.[[450]](#cite_note-457)

Notable sportspeople include Commonwealth Games gold medalist [Michael East](/wiki/Michael_East_(athlete) "Michael East (athlete)"),[[451]](#cite_note-458) Olympic medallist in cycling [Rob Hayles](/wiki/Rob_Hayles "Rob Hayles"),[[452]](#cite_note-459) former British light-heavyweight boxing champion [Tony Oakey](/wiki/Tony_Oakey "Tony Oakey"),[[453]](#cite_note-460) Olympic medallist [Alan Pascoe](/wiki/Alan_Pascoe "Alan Pascoe") as well as professional footballer [Mason Mount](/wiki/Mason_Mount "Mason Mount").[[454]](#cite_note-461) Single-handed yachtsman [Alec Rose](/wiki/Alec_Rose "Alec Rose"),[[455]](#cite_note-462) [2003 World Aquatics Championships](/wiki/2003_World_Aquatics_Championships "2003 World Aquatics Championships") gold medallist [Katy Sexton](/wiki/Katy_Sexton "Katy Sexton"),[[456]](#cite_note-463) and Olympic medallist [Roger Black](/wiki/Roger_Black "Roger Black") were also born in the city.[[457]](#cite_note-464) [Jamshid bin Abdullah of Zanzibar](/wiki/Jamshid_bin_Abdullah_of_Zanzibar "Jamshid bin Abdullah of Zanzibar"), the last constitutional monarch of the island state, lived in exile in Portsmouth with his wife and six children,[[458]](#cite_note-465) prior to resettling in his ancestral land of [Oman](/wiki/Oman "Oman").[[459]](#cite_note-466)
